title: Digital Electronics
description: Digital refresh + modernize for EE/radar/software: 14 sub-topics from number systems through HDL and FPGAs. Each lesson has a reading, LaTeX/code, and diagrams; tiered easy to hard.

lessons.title: Positional Notation, Radix & Base Conversion
lessons.content:
Every number system you use is **positional**: a digit's weight is the radix raised to its position index. In radix $r$, the value of a string is $\sum_{i} d_i \cdot r^{i}$, where $i$ counts positions from $0$ at the right. Decimal is just $r=10$; binary is $r=2$; octal $r=8$; hex $r=16$. So $\mathtt{1011}_2 = 1\cdot2^3 + 0\cdot2^2 + 1\cdot2^1 + 1\cdot2^0 = 11_{10}$.

**Hex is the human-readable face of binary** because $16 = 2^4$: every hex digit maps to exactly one nibble (4 bits), so you regroup bits four at a time with zero arithmetic. $\mathtt{0xB7} = \mathtt{1011\,0111}_2$. Octal ($2^3$) does the same in 3-bit groups and survives mainly in Unix file permissions. To go **binary to hex**, pad to a multiple of 4 bits and translate each group; to go **hex to binary**, expand each digit to 4 bits.

Converting **decimal to binary** uses repeated division: divide by 2, record remainders, read them bottom-up. Going the other way, just sum the set bit-weights. A bit is one binary digit; a **nibble** is 4 bits, a **byte** is 8, and a **word** is whatever your architecture calls native (16/32/64). **Endianness** describes byte ordering in memory: big-endian stores the most-significant byte first, little-endian the least-significant first. The value $\mathtt{0x12345678}$ on a little-endian machine sits in memory as bytes $\mathtt{78\,56\,34\,12}$ — a classic source of bugs when you cast a pointer or read a network packet.

items.front: In radix $r$, what is the value of a digit string $d_n \dots d_1 d_0$?
items.back: $\sum_{i=0}^{n} d_i \cdot r^{i}$ — each digit is weighted by the radix raised to its position index (counting from 0 at the right).
items.hint: Position index is the exponent on the base.

items.front: Why is hexadecimal the natural shorthand for binary?
items.back: Because $16 = 2^4$: each hex digit corresponds to exactly one 4-bit nibble, so converting is pure regrouping with no arithmetic.
items.hint: Think about $16$ as a power of 2.

items.front: Convert $\mathtt{1011\,0111}_2$ to hexadecimal.
items.back: $\mathtt{1011} = \mathtt{B}$, $\mathtt{0111} = 7$, so the value is $\mathtt{0xB7}$.
items.hint: Split into nibbles and translate each.

items.front: What is the repeated-division method for decimal-to-binary conversion?
items.back: Repeatedly divide by 2, recording each remainder (0 or 1). Read the remainders from last to first (bottom-up) to get the binary digits.
items.hint: Remainders, read in reverse.

items.front: Define big-endian vs little-endian byte ordering.
items.back: Big-endian stores the most-significant byte at the lowest address; little-endian stores the least-significant byte first. E.g. $\mathtt{0x12345678}$ little-endian = bytes $\mathtt{78\,56\,34\,12}$.
items.hint: Which byte lands at the lowest address?

items.prompt: What is the decimal value of $\mathtt{1011}_2$?
items.explanation: $1\cdot8 + 0\cdot4 + 1\cdot2 + 1\cdot1 = 11$.

items.prompt: How many binary bits does a single hexadecimal digit represent?
items.explanation: $16 = 2^4$, so each hex digit maps to exactly 4 bits (one nibble).

items.prompt: When converting binary to octal, bits are grouped in sets of how many?
items.explanation: $8 = 2^3$, so octal groups bits three at a time, in contrast to hex's four.

items.prompt: A little-endian machine stores the 32-bit value $\mathtt{0x0A0B0C0D}$. Which byte is at the lowest memory address?
items.explanation: Little-endian places the least-significant byte ($\mathtt{0x0D}$) at the lowest address.

lessons.title: Binary Arithmetic, Signed Representations & Overflow
lessons.content:
Binary addition works column-by-column with carries, exactly like decimal: $1+1 = \mathtt{10}$ (write 0, carry 1). The interesting question is how to represent **negatives**. Three schemes compete. **Sign-magnitude** dedicates the MSB to sign and leaves the rest as magnitude — simple to read but it has two zeros ($+0$ and $-0$) and needs separate add/subtract logic. **One's complement** negates by flipping every bit; it also has a $\pm0$ problem and an awkward end-around carry.

**Two's complement won** because it has a single zero and ordinary binary addition "just works" across the sign boundary — the same adder hardware handles signed and unsigned. To negate, invert all bits and add 1: $-x = \overline{x} + 1$. For an $n$-bit word the representable range is $[-2^{n-1},\ 2^{n-1}-1]$ — asymmetric, with one extra negative value. The MSB carries weight $-2^{n-1}$, so $\mathtt{1000}_2 = -8$ in 4 bits.

**Overflow detection** is where ex-hardware folks must stay sharp: **carry-out and signed overflow are different flags**. Carry-out (C) is the carry beyond the MSB and signals *unsigned* overflow. Signed **overflow (V)** occurs when two same-sign operands produce a wrong-sign result; equivalently, $V = C_{in} \oplus C_{out}$ at the MSB. Adding $+5 + +4$ in 4 bits gives $\mathtt{1001} = -7$: V is set, C is clear. **Sign extension** preserves value when widening: replicate the MSB into the new high bits ($\mathtt{1011} \to \mathtt{1111\,1011}$).

items.front: How do you negate a two's-complement number?
items.back: Invert every bit and add 1: $-x = \overline{x} + 1$. The same operation applied twice returns the original value.
items.hint: Complement, then increment.

items.front: Why did two's complement win over sign-magnitude and one's complement?
items.back: It has a single representation of zero, and ordinary binary addition works correctly across the sign boundary — one adder handles both signed and unsigned arithmetic.
items.hint: Think about zero and reusing the adder.

items.front: What is the representable range of an $n$-bit two's-complement integer?
items.back: $[-2^{n-1},\ 2^{n-1}-1]$. It is asymmetric: one more negative value than positive because of the single zero.
items.hint: The MSB has weight $-2^{n-1}$.

items.front: Give a formula for the signed overflow (V) flag in an adder.
items.back: $V = C_{in} \oplus C_{out}$ at the most-significant bit. Equivalently, V is set when two same-sign operands yield a result of the opposite sign.
items.hint: XOR of the two carries into and out of the MSB.

items.front: How do you sign-extend a two's-complement number to a wider word?
items.back: Replicate the sign bit (MSB) into all the new high-order bits. E.g. $\mathtt{1011}$ (=-5) extends to $\mathtt{1111\,1011}$.
items.hint: Copy the MSB upward.

items.prompt: What decimal value does $\mathtt{1000}_2$ represent as a 4-bit two's-complement number?
items.explanation: The MSB weight is $-2^3 = -8$ and all other bits are 0, so the value is $-8$ — the most negative 4-bit value.

items.prompt: Adding 4-bit two's-complement $0101 (+5)$ and $0100 (+4)$ yields $1001$. Which flags are correct?
items.explanation: Two positives produced a negative result ($-7$): signed overflow V is set, but no carry leaves the MSB so C is clear.

items.prompt: Which statement about carry-out (C) and signed overflow (V) is correct?
choices: C signals unsigned overflow; V signals signed overflow
V signals unsigned overflow; C signals signed overflow
Only V exists in two's complement
items.explanation: Carry-out indicates the result exceeded the unsigned range; V indicates the signed result has the wrong sign. They are independent flags.

items.prompt: In 8-bit two's complement, what is the representation of $-1$?
items.explanation: Negate $+1$ ($\mathtt{0000\,0001}$): invert to $\mathtt{1111\,1110}$, add 1 to get $\mathtt{1111\,1111}$.

lessons.title: Fixed-Point (Q-Format), BCD & Gray Code
lessons.content:
When you need fractions but cannot afford floating-point hardware — the daily reality of **DSP and radar signal processing [→08]** — you use **fixed-point**. The bits are still a plain integer; you merely *agree* on an implied binary point. **Q-format** names the split: **Q$m$.$n$** has $m$ integer bits and $n$ fraction bits, with the fraction contributing weights $2^{-1}, 2^{-2}, \dots, 2^{-n}$. The ubiquitous **Q1.15** packs a sign bit and 15 fraction bits into 16 bits, representing values in $[-1,\ 1)$ with resolution $2^{-15}$.

The key discipline is tracking the format through operations. **Addition** requires identical Q-formats and keeps the format. **Multiplication adds the formats**: a Q1.15 $\times$ Q1.15 product is Q2.30 in a 32-bit register, which you typically shift left by 1 and take the high word to renormalize back to Q1.15. Forgetting that re-scale is the classic fixed-point bug. **Saturating arithmetic** clamps out-of-range results to the max/min instead of **wrapping** (modulo rollover) — essential in signal chains where a wrap turns a loud sample into a catastrophic sign flip.

Two special codes round out the picture. **Binary-coded decimal (BCD)** stores each decimal digit in its own nibble ($\mathtt{0010\,0111} = 27$), trading density for trivial decimal display — it survives in 7-segment displays and real-time clocks. **Gray code** changes exactly one bit between adjacent values, eliminating transition glitches; it is the encoding of choice for rotary/position encoders and for multi-bit clock-domain crossing **[→06]**. Convert binary $b$ to Gray with $g = b \oplus (b \gg 1)$.

items.front: What does the notation Q$m$.$n$ mean in fixed-point arithmetic?
items.back: $m$ integer bits and $n$ fraction bits, with an implied binary point between them. The fraction bits carry weights $2^{-1}$ through $2^{-n}$.
items.hint: Integer part dot fraction part.

items.front: What range and resolution does Q1.15 provide?
items.back: 16 bits = 1 sign bit + 15 fraction bits, representing $[-1,\ 1)$ with resolution (LSB) of $2^{-15}$.
items.hint: It's a normalized signed fraction.

items.front: What is the output format of a Q1.15 $\times$ Q1.15 multiplication, and what must you do?
items.back: The raw product is Q2.30 in 32 bits. To return to Q1.15 you shift left by 1 (to drop the redundant sign bit) and take the high 16 bits — i.e. re-scale.
items.hint: Formats add on multiply; then renormalize.

items.front: Contrast saturating and wrapping arithmetic.
items.back: Saturating clamps an out-of-range result to the maximum or minimum value; wrapping lets it roll over modulo the word size. DSP prefers saturation to avoid catastrophic sign flips.
items.hint: Clamp vs roll over.

items.front: What is binary-coded decimal (BCD) and where does it survive?
items.back: Each decimal digit is stored in its own 4-bit nibble (e.g. $27 = \mathtt{0010\,0111}$). It survives in 7-segment displays and real-time clocks, where direct decimal readout matters more than density.
items.hint: One nibble per decimal digit.

items.front: How do you convert a binary number $b$ to Gray code, and why use Gray code?
items.back: $g = b \oplus (b \gg 1)$. Gray code changes only one bit between adjacent values, eliminating multi-bit transition glitches in encoders and clock-domain crossings.
items.hint: XOR with the right-shifted value.

items.prompt: Multiplying two Q1.15 numbers produces a raw product in which format?
items.explanation: Integer and fraction bit counts each add: $1+1=2$ integer bits, $15+15=30$ fraction bits, giving Q2.30 in a 32-bit register.

items.prompt: What decimal number does the BCD byte $\mathtt{1001\,0011}$ represent?
items.explanation: Each nibble is one decimal digit: $\mathtt{1001}=9$ and $\mathtt{0011}=3$, so the value is 93 (not the binary value 147).

items.prompt: What is the defining property of Gray code that makes it ideal for encoders and CDC?
choices: It is the densest possible code
Adjacent values differ in exactly one bit
It has no representation of zero
It uses fewer bits than binary
items.explanation: Only one bit changes between consecutive Gray values, so a sampled transition can never catch an intermediate multi-bit glitch.

items.prompt: In an 8-bit unsigned saturating adder, what is $200 + 100$?
items.explanation: 300 exceeds the 8-bit max of 255, so saturating arithmetic clamps the result to 255 rather than wrapping to 44.

lessons.title: IEEE 754 Floating-Point
lessons.content:
Floating-point trades uniform resolution for enormous dynamic range by storing numbers in scientific notation. **IEEE 754 single precision** packs 32 bits as a $1$-bit **sign** $S$, an $8$-bit biased **exponent** $E$, and a $23$-bit **mantissa** (fraction) $M$. For normalized numbers the value is

$$x = (-1)^{S} \times 1.M \times 2^{(E - 127)}$$

The leading $1$ is **implicit** (the "hidden bit"), buying a free 24th bit of precision. The exponent uses a **bias** of $127$ (single) or $1023$ (double) so the stored field stays unsigned while representing both positive and negative exponents. Double precision is the same scheme with $11$ exponent bits and a $52$-bit mantissa.

The exponent field's extremes are reserved. $E = 0$ encodes **zero** (with $M=0$) and **denormals/subnormals** (with $M \ne 0$), which drop the implicit 1 to represent values gradually underflowing toward zero. $E = 255$ (all ones) encodes $\pm\infty$ (with $M=0$) and **NaN** (Not-a-Number, $M \ne 0$), the result of $0/0$ or $\sqrt{-1}$. NaN famously compares unequal to everything, including itself.

Because the mantissa is binary, decimal fractions like $0.1$ have **no exact representation** — $0.1$ is a repeating binary fraction, so it is rounded to the nearest representable value, which is why $0.1 + 0.2 \ne 0.3$ in float. The default rounding mode is **round-to-nearest, ties-to-even**. As a returning engineer, the practical takeaways are: never test floats for exact equality, watch for catastrophic cancellation when subtracting near-equal values, and reach for fixed-point when you need deterministic, exact-scaled arithmetic on an FPGA.

items.front: What are the three fields of an IEEE 754 single-precision number and their widths?
items.back: Sign $S$ (1 bit), biased exponent $E$ (8 bits), and mantissa/fraction $M$ (23 bits), for 32 bits total.

items.front: Write the value formula for a normalized IEEE 754 single-precision number.
items.back: $x = (-1)^{S} \times 1.M \times 2^{(E - 127)}$. The leading 1 is implicit and the exponent bias is 127.
items.hint: Hidden bit and bias of 127.

items.front: Why does IEEE 754 store the exponent with a bias instead of two's complement?
items.back: Bias keeps the stored exponent field unsigned (so float magnitude comparison can use integer comparison), while still representing both positive and negative exponents. Single uses bias 127, double uses 1023.
items.hint: Keeps the field unsigned and orderable.

items.front: How are $\pm\infty$ and NaN encoded in IEEE 754?
items.back: Exponent field all ones ($E=255$ in single): mantissa $M=0$ gives $\pm\infty$, while $M \ne 0$ gives NaN. NaN compares unequal to everything, including itself.
items.hint: Max exponent; mantissa zero vs nonzero.

items.front: What do denormal (subnormal) numbers represent and how are they encoded?
items.back: $E = 0$ with $M \ne 0$. They drop the implicit leading 1, allowing gradual underflow — representing very small magnitudes below the smallest normalized value down toward zero.
items.hint: Exponent field zero, nonzero mantissa, no hidden bit.

items.front: Why is $0.1$ not exactly representable in binary floating-point?
items.back: $0.1$ is a repeating fraction in binary (like $1/3$ in decimal), so it is rounded to the nearest representable value. This is why $0.1 + 0.2 \ne 0.3$ exactly.
items.hint: Repeating binary expansion, then rounding.

items.prompt: How many exponent bits and what bias does IEEE 754 double precision use?
items.explanation: Double precision has 11 exponent bits with a bias of 1023, plus a 52-bit mantissa and 1 sign bit (64 bits total).

items.prompt: The 'hidden bit' in normalized IEEE 754 numbers refers to what?
choices: An implicit leading 1 before the mantissa's binary point
items.explanation: Normalized values are $1.M$; the leading 1 is implied and not stored, giving 24 bits of effective precision in single from a 23-bit field.

items.prompt: Which expression is true for an IEEE 754 NaN value x?
items.explanation: NaN is unordered: every comparison except inequality returns false, so $x == x$ is false. This is the standard way to detect NaN.

items.prompt: What is the default IEEE 754 rounding mode?
items.explanation: Round-to-nearest, ties-to-even (banker's rounding) is the default; it minimizes statistical bias when ties occur.

lessons.title: Boolean Variables, Operators & Truth Tables
lessons.content:
Boolean algebra is the grammar of digital design: every variable lives in $\{0, 1\}$, and three primitive operators generate everything else. **NOT** (complement) inverts: $\overline{A}$ is $1$ when $A$ is $0$. **AND** (conjunction, written $A \cdot B$ or $AB$) is $1$ only when *both* inputs are $1$. **OR** (disjunction, $A + B$) is $1$ when *at least one* input is $1$. The overbar, dot, and plus are the working notation you will see on schematics and in HDL comments alike.

From these three we derive the operators that hardware actually leans on. **NAND** is $\overline{A \cdot B}$, **NOR** is $\overline{A + B}$. **XOR** (exclusive-OR) is true when inputs *differ*: $A \oplus B = \overline{A}B + A\overline{B}$ — the bit-difference detector, and the heart of adders, parity, and comparators. **XNOR** ($\overline{A \oplus B}$) is the equality detector.

A **truth table** is the exhaustive ground truth for a function: list all $2^n$ input combinations for $n$ variables and record the output. It is the unambiguous specification a synthesis tool starts from, and the final arbiter when you want to *prove* two expressions are equal — just confirm identical output columns.

Note the two conventions: in **positive logic**, the higher voltage is $1$; in **negative logic**, the lower voltage is $1$. The algebra is identical; only the physical mapping flips. As a radar tech you already had this in your hands as active-high vs active-low control lines.

items.front: Write the three primitive Boolean operators and their notation.
items.back: NOT (complement): $\overline{A}$. AND (conjunction): $A \cdot B$ or $AB$. OR (disjunction): $A + B$. All variables take values in $\{0,1\}$.
items.hint: Overbar, dot, plus.

items.front: Express XOR in terms of AND, OR, and NOT, and say in words when it is 1.
items.back: $A \oplus B = \overline{A}B + A\overline{B}$. It is $1$ exactly when the two inputs **differ** — a bit-difference detector used in adders, parity, and comparators.
items.hint: True when inputs differ.

items.front: Define NAND and NOR as expressions.
items.back: NAND is $\overline{A \cdot B}$ (AND then invert). NOR is $\overline{A + B}$ (OR then invert). Each is the complement of its base operator.
items.hint: Invert the AND / invert the OR.

items.front: What does XNOR detect, and how is it written?
items.back: XNOR is $\overline{A \oplus B}$; it outputs $1$ when the inputs are **equal**. It is the equality (coincidence) detector, the building block of comparators.
items.hint: Complement of XOR; equality.

items.front: What is a truth table and why is it the canonical specification of a logic function?
items.back: A table listing the output for all $2^n$ combinations of $n$ inputs. It is exhaustive and unambiguous, so it is what synthesis starts from and the way to prove two expressions equal: identical output columns means identical functions.
items.hint: All $2^n$ rows; output column.

items.front: Distinguish positive-logic from negative-logic conventions.
items.back: In positive logic the higher voltage represents $1$; in negative logic the lower voltage represents $1$. The Boolean algebra is unchanged — only the voltage-to-symbol mapping flips (active-high vs active-low signals).
items.hint: Which voltage means 1.

items.prompt: For inputs $A=1$, $B=0$, what does $A \oplus B$ evaluate to?
items.explanation: XOR is $1$ when the inputs differ. $A=1$ and $B=0$ differ, so $A \oplus B = 1$. The result is independent of positive/negative logic convention at the algebraic level.

items.prompt: How many rows does the truth table of a 4-input Boolean function have?
items.explanation: A function of $n$ inputs has $2^n$ input combinations. For $n=4$, that is $2^4 = 16$ rows.

items.prompt: Which expression is equal to $\overline{A}B + A\overline{B}$?
items.explanation: $\overline{A}B + A\overline{B}$ is true exactly when the inputs differ, which is the definition of exclusive-OR, $A \oplus B$.

lessons.title: Axioms, Theorems & the Duality Principle
lessons.content:
Boolean algebra rests on a small set of axioms, from which every simplification follows. The pairs you must own reflexively: **identity** ($A + 0 = A$, $A \cdot 1 = A$), **null/annihilator** ($A + 1 = 1$, $A \cdot 0 = 0$), **idempotence** ($A + A = A$, $A \cdot A = A$), and **complement** ($A + \overline{A} = 1$, $A \cdot \overline{A} = 0$). Layered on top are **commutativity**, **associativity**, and **distributivity** — and crucially, *both* distributive laws hold: $A(B + C) = AB + AC$ and the one with no arithmetic analog, $A + BC = (A + B)(A + C)$.

Two higher-level theorems do real work in simplification. **Absorption**: $A + AB = A$ and $A(A + B) = A$ — a term is swallowed by a broader one. **Consensus**: $AB + \overline{A}C + BC = AB + \overline{A}C$; the $BC$ term is redundant (yet, kept deliberately, it suppresses hazards — a Module 5 callback).

The **duality principle** is the great labor-saver: every Boolean identity has a dual obtained by swapping $\cdot \leftrightarrow +$ and $0 \leftrightarrow 1$ while leaving variables alone. Prove one form and you get its dual for free — which is exactly why the axioms above come in pairs. Note duality is a statement about *identities*; it is not the same as complementation, though De Morgan (next lesson) connects the two.

items.front: State the identity and null (annihilator) laws.
items.back: Identity: $A + 0 = A$ and $A \cdot 1 = A$. Null/annihilator: $A + 1 = 1$ and $A \cdot 0 = 0$. These come in dual pairs.
items.hint: 0 and 1 as neutral / dominant elements.

items.front: State the complement and idempotence laws.
items.back: Complement: $A + \overline{A} = 1$ and $A \cdot \overline{A} = 0$. Idempotence: $A + A = A$ and $A \cdot A = A$. (No squaring or doubling — Boolean values are binary.)
items.hint: A with its own complement / itself.

items.front: Give both distributive laws, noting which has no ordinary-arithmetic analog.
items.back: $A(B + C) = AB + AC$ (familiar), and $A + BC = (A + B)(A + C)$ (the OR-over-AND form, which has no analog in ordinary algebra).
items.hint: Two forms; one is unusual.

items.front: State the absorption law in both forms.
items.back: $A + AB = A$ and $A(A + B) = A$. The more specific term ($AB$) is absorbed by the broader one ($A$).
items.hint: A swallows AB.

items.front: State the consensus theorem and note its hazard-removal twist.
items.back: $AB + \overline{A}C + BC = AB + \overline{A}C$: the consensus term $BC$ is redundant for the logic value. However, deliberately keeping it can suppress static hazards in real gate networks.
items.hint: BC is the redundant consensus term.

items.front: State the duality principle and how to form a dual.
items.back: Every Boolean identity has a valid dual obtained by swapping $\cdot \leftrightarrow +$ and $0 \leftrightarrow 1$, leaving variables unchanged. Prove one and the dual holds automatically — which is why the axioms come in pairs.
items.hint: Swap AND/OR and 0/1.

items.prompt: Simplify $A + AB$.
items.explanation: By absorption, $A + AB = A(1 + B) = A \cdot 1 = A$. The $AB$ term is fully covered by $A$.

items.prompt: What is the dual of the identity $A + 1 = 1$?
items.explanation: Form the dual by swapping $+ \to \cdot$ and $1 \to 0$: $A + 1 = 1$ becomes $A \cdot 0 = 0$. Variables are left unchanged.

items.prompt: Which term is redundant in $AB + \overline{A}C + BC$ by the consensus theorem?
items.explanation: The consensus of $AB$ and $\overline{A}C$ (over the variable $A$) is $BC$, which is logically redundant: $AB + \overline{A}C + BC = AB + \overline{A}C$.

items.prompt: Apply the OR-over-AND distributive law to $A + BC$.
items.explanation: The second distributive law states $A + BC = (A + B)(A + C)$. This form has no counterpart in ordinary arithmetic but is valid in Boolean algebra.

lessons.title: De Morgan's Theorems & Functional Completeness
lessons.content:
**De Morgan's theorems** are the workhorses of digital design: $\overline{A \cdot B} = \overline{A} + \overline{B}$ and $\overline{A + B} = \overline{A} \cdot \overline{B}$. In words: complement the whole expression by complementing each variable and swapping AND with OR. They generalize to any number of variables and let you push bubbles through a network freely — "break the bar, change the sign." Graphically, a NAND is equivalent to an OR with inverted inputs, and a NOR equals an AND with inverted inputs (see diagram). This is how you re-read any gate schematic into the form that matches your available parts.

De Morgan underwrites **functional completeness**: a set of operators is complete if it can build *every* Boolean function. $\{$AND, OR, NOT$\}$ is complete by construction (any truth table becomes a sum of products). The remarkable result is that **NAND alone is complete**, and so is **NOR alone**. To see it: $\overline{A} = \overline{A \cdot A}$ (a NAND with tied inputs gives NOT); $A \cdot B = \overline{\overline{A \cdot B}}$ (NAND followed by an inverting NAND gives AND); and OR follows from De Morgan, $A + B = \overline{\overline{A} \cdot \overline{B}}$, built from three NANDs.

This is *why* real CMOS chips are seas of NAND (and NOR) gates: a single universal cell, replicated, implements anything — simplifying fabrication, characterization, and the standard-cell libraries that synthesis tools target.

items.front: State both of De Morgan's theorems.
items.back: $\overline{A \cdot B} = \overline{A} + \overline{B}$ and $\overline{A + B} = \overline{A} \cdot \overline{B}$. Complement each variable and swap AND with OR.
items.hint: Break the bar, change the operator.

items.front: Graphically, what is a NAND gate equivalent to under De Morgan?
items.back: A NAND ($\overline{A \cdot B}$) equals an OR gate with both inputs inverted: $\overline{A \cdot B} = \overline{A} + \overline{B}$. Likewise a NOR equals an AND with inverted inputs.
items.hint: Bubbled-input OR.

items.front: What does it mean for a set of logic operators to be functionally complete?
items.back: The set can express every possible Boolean function. $\{\text{AND, OR, NOT}\}$ is complete; remarkably, $\{\text{NAND}\}$ alone and $\{\text{NOR}\}$ alone are each complete (universal) as well.
items.hint: Can build any truth table.

items.front: How do you make a NOT gate from a single NAND?
items.back: Tie the inputs together: $\overline{A \cdot A} = \overline{A}$. A 2-input NAND with both inputs driven by $A$ outputs the complement of $A$.
items.hint: Tie both inputs to the same signal.

items.front: How do you build OR from NAND gates only?
items.back: By De Morgan, $A + B = \overline{\overline{A} \cdot \overline{B}}$. Invert each input with a NAND-as-NOT, then feed both into a third NAND: three NANDs total.
items.hint: Invert inputs, then NAND them.

items.front: Why are real CMOS chips built largely from NAND and NOR cells?
items.back: Because NAND (or NOR) alone is functionally complete, a single universal standard cell, replicated, can implement any logic. This simplifies fabrication, characterization, and the standard-cell library that synthesis targets.
items.hint: One universal cell builds everything.

items.prompt: Apply De Morgan to simplify $\overline{A + B}$.
items.explanation: $\overline{A + B} = \overline{A} \cdot \overline{B}$: complement each variable and change OR to AND. This is the second De Morgan theorem.

items.prompt: What is the minimum number of 2-input NAND gates needed to build a 2-input OR gate?
items.explanation: $A + B = \overline{\overline{A} \cdot \overline{B}}$. Two NANDs act as inverters for $A$ and $B$, and a third NANDs the results — three gates total.

items.prompt: Which statement about functional completeness is correct?
choices: NAND alone can implement any Boolean function
Only the full {AND, OR, NOT} set is complete
items.explanation: NAND (like NOR) is a universal gate: NOT, AND, and OR can all be derived from NAND, so it alone is functionally complete. XOR and OR alone are not (they cannot produce a constant or a complement without help).

lessons.title: Canonical Forms, SOP/POS & Don't-Cares
lessons.content:
Any function has two **canonical forms** read straight off its truth table. **Sum of Products (SOP)** ORs together one **minterm** per row where the output is $1$; a minterm is the AND of all variables, each true or complemented to match that row. **Product of Sums (POS)** ANDs together one **maxterm** per row where the output is $0$; a maxterm is the OR of all variables, complemented to match. Minterms are numbered by reading the row's input bits as a binary index: for $f(A,B,C)$, the row $A{=}1,B{=}0,C{=}1$ is $m_5 = A\overline{B}C$. We write, e.g., $f = \sum m(1,3,5,7)$ for SOP or $f = \prod M(0,2,4,6)$ for POS — and the index sets are complementary.

Canonical forms are *correct* but rarely *minimal*; algebra or K-maps (Module 5) reduce them. The reduction lever that beginners miss is the **don't-care** condition, written $X$ or $d$: an input combination that physically cannot occur, or whose output nobody reads. Because its output is unconstrained, you may assign it $0$ or $1$ — whichever yields larger groupings and a cheaper circuit. The classic case is a BCD decoder, where inputs $1010$ through $1111$ never occur, freeing six don't-cares to simplify the logic.

Used correctly, don't-cares cut gate count, power, and delay for free — the same optimization a synthesizer performs when you leave a `default` unspecified. Used carelessly (assuming a "can't-happen" input truly can't) they are a classic source of field failures.

items.front: Define a minterm and how the canonical SOP form is built.
items.back: A minterm is the AND of all input variables, each complemented or not to make that one row's inputs evaluate to $1$. Canonical SOP ORs together the minterms for every row whose output is $1$.
items.hint: AND of all variables; OR the 1-rows.

items.front: Define a maxterm and how the canonical POS form is built.
items.back: A maxterm is the OR of all input variables, each complemented or not so that one row's inputs evaluate to $0$. Canonical POS ANDs together the maxterms for every row whose output is $0$.
items.hint: OR of all variables; AND the 0-rows.

items.front: How are minterms numbered? Give $m_5$ for $f(A,B,C)$.
items.back: Read the row's input bits as a binary number (MSB first). For $f(A,B,C)$, index $5 = 101_2$ means $A{=}1, B{=}0, C{=}1$, so $m_5 = A\overline{B}C$.
items.hint: Binary value of the input row.

items.front: How do the minterm and maxterm index sets of one function relate?
items.back: They are complementary: every row index is either a minterm (output $1$) for the SOP or a maxterm (output $0$) for the POS. If $f = \sum m(1,3,5,7)$ on three variables, then $f = \prod M(0,2,4,6)$.
items.hint: 1-rows vs 0-rows partition all indices.

items.front: What is a don't-care condition and why is it useful?
items.back: An input combination that cannot occur or whose output is never used, written $X$ or $d$. Since its output is unconstrained, you may assign $0$ or $1$ to maximize groupings, yielding a cheaper (fewer gates, less delay) circuit.
items.hint: Free to pick 0 or 1.

items.front: Give a concrete example of where don't-cares arise.
items.back: A BCD (binary-coded decimal) circuit: the 4-bit inputs $1010$ through $1111$ (decimal 10-15) never represent a valid digit, so those six combinations are don't-cares available to simplify the logic.
items.hint: Invalid BCD codes 10 through 15.

items.prompt: For $f(A,B,C)$, what is minterm $m_6$?
items.explanation: $6 = 110_2$, so $A{=}1, B{=}1, C{=}0$. The minterm uses each variable true or complemented to match: $A B \overline{C}$.

items.prompt: A 3-variable function has canonical SOP $f = \sum m(0,2,5,7)$. Which is its canonical POS index set?
items.explanation: POS maxterms are the rows where $f=0$. With 3 variables the indices run 0-7; the complement of $\{0,2,5,7\}$ is $\{1,3,4,6\}$, so $f = \prod M(1,3,4,6)$.

items.prompt: What is the correct way to treat a don't-care during minimization?
choices: Assign it 0 or 1, whichever produces larger groupings and simpler logic
Leave it out of the cover entirely, never grouping it
items.explanation: Don't-cares are unconstrained, so you choose $0$ or $1$ for each to form the largest possible groups (prime implicants), minimizing gate count. You include them only when doing so helps.

items.prompt: Why are canonical SOP/POS forms usually not the final implementation?
choices: They are correct but not minimal; they use far more gates than a reduced form
They can represent only a subset of functions
They cannot be derived from a truth table
They are ambiguous and may not match the truth table
items.explanation: Canonical forms list every minterm/maxterm verbatim, so they are exact but redundant. Algebraic simplification or K-maps reduce them to fewer gates, lower power, and shorter delay.

lessons.title: CMOS Gate Construction: Pull-Up and Pull-Down Networks
lessons.content:
Modern logic is **CMOS** (complementary MOS): every gate is built from a **pull-up network (PUN)** of PMOS transistors that connects the output to $V_{DD}$, and a **pull-down network (PDN)** of NMOS transistors that connects it to ground. The two networks are logical complements, so for any input combination exactly one of them conducts — the output is always driven hard to a rail, never left floating.

The foundation is the **inverter**: a low input turns the PMOS on (output pulled to $V_{DD}$, a logic HIGH) and the NMOS off; a high input does the reverse. Because one device is always off, there is no steady DC path from rail to rail, so static current is essentially zero.

A key asymmetry from your radar-tech intuition about device polarity: **NMOS passes a strong 0, PMOS passes a strong 1**. That is why the PDN (which must produce a solid LOW) uses NMOS and the PUN (solid HIGH) uses PMOS. Series transistors implement AND-like conduction; parallel transistors implement OR-like conduction.

This makes **NAND and NOR the natural CMOS gates**. A 2-input NAND uses two series NMOS in the PDN and two parallel PMOS in the PUN — four transistors. A NOR swaps the topology. An **AND or OR is more expensive**: you build the NAND/NOR and then add an inverter (two more transistors). This is why synthesizers and standard-cell libraries lean on inverting logic, and why functional completeness with NAND/NOR (Module 2) is not just theory but the physical reality of silicon.

items.front: In a CMOS gate, what are the pull-up and pull-down networks made of, and what do they connect to?
items.back: The pull-up network (PUN) is PMOS transistors connecting the output to V_DD; the pull-down network (PDN) is NMOS transistors connecting the output to ground. They are logical complements, so exactly one conducts for any input.
items.hint: PMOS to the high rail, NMOS to the low rail.

items.front: Why does the pull-down use NMOS and the pull-up use PMOS, and not the reverse?
items.back: NMOS passes a strong 0 (poor 1), and PMOS passes a strong 1 (poor 0). So NMOS in the PDN delivers a solid LOW and PMOS in the PUN delivers a solid HIGH, giving full rail-to-rail output swing.
items.hint: Each device type passes one logic level well.

items.front: How do series vs. parallel transistors map to Boolean operations in a CMOS network?
items.back: Series transistors conduct only when all are on (AND-like); parallel transistors conduct when any is on (OR-like). The PDN and PUN use dual (complementary) series/parallel topologies.
items.hint: Both must be on vs. either one on.

items.front: Why are NAND and NOR the 'natural' CMOS gates while AND and OR cost more?
items.back: The PUN/PDN structure inherently inverts, so a 2-input NAND or NOR is just 4 transistors. AND/OR require that same gate plus an output inverter (2 more transistors), so they are larger and slower.
items.hint: CMOS inverts naturally; un-inverting costs an inverter.

items.front: How many transistors does a 2-input CMOS NAND use, and how is it arranged?
items.back: Four: two NMOS in series in the pull-down (output LOW only when both inputs HIGH) and two PMOS in parallel in the pull-up (output HIGH if either input LOW).
items.hint: Series NMOS, parallel PMOS.

items.prompt: In a static CMOS gate, why is there essentially no DC current from V_DD to ground in a stable logic state?
choices: A series resistor limits the current
Either the pull-up or pull-down network is off, breaking the path
items.explanation: Because the PUN and PDN are complementary, in any stable state one of them is fully off, so no continuous path exists between the rails. Current flows mainly during transitions.

items.prompt: To build a 2-input CMOS NOR, the pull-down network is:
items.explanation: NOR is LOW when either input is HIGH, so the NMOS pull-down is two devices in parallel; the PMOS pull-up is two in series (HIGH only when both inputs are LOW).

items.prompt: A standard-cell synthesizer tends to favor NAND/NOR-based logic primarily because:
choices: They are easier to name in HDL
Inverting gates are smaller and faster in CMOS, so AND/OR are decomposed into them
They consume more static power, which improves drive
items.explanation: CMOS naturally produces inverting gates with the fewest transistors and least delay. AND/OR add an inverter, so tools map logic onto NAND/NOR/inverter cells for area and speed.

items.prompt: Which statement about NMOS and PMOS pass behavior is correct?
choices: NMOS passes a strong 1, PMOS a strong 0
NMOS passes a strong 0, PMOS a strong 1
items.explanation: An NMOS source can only fall to within a threshold of its gate, so it passes 0 well and 1 poorly; PMOS is the opposite. This dictates PDN=NMOS, PUN=PMOS.

items.front: CMOS inverter topology (PMOS pull-up over NMOS pull-down)
items.back: The shared gate is the input. A LOW input turns the PMOS on and NMOS off, pulling OUT to V_DD (HIGH). A HIGH input turns the NMOS on and PMOS off, pulling OUT to ground (LOW). One device is always off, so static current is near zero.

lessons.title: Static vs. Dynamic Power and P = CV²f
lessons.content:
CMOS power has two regimes. **Static (leakage) power** is what flows when nothing is switching: subthreshold leakage, gate-oxide tunneling, and junction leakage. In older, larger-geometry CMOS this was negligible, which is the historical selling point. In deep-submicron nodes, leakage has become a first-order design problem (the reason for power gating and high-$V_t$ cells), but for board-level 74-series logic it is still tiny.

**Dynamic power** dominates when the circuit clocks. Each time an output toggles, the load capacitance $C$ is charged to $V_{DD}$ and then discharged. Charging $C$ draws energy $C V_{DD}^2$ from the supply, of which exactly half, $\tfrac{1}{2}C V_{DD}^2$, is dissipated in the PMOS and the other half later in the NMOS. With a switching frequency $f$ (and activity factor $\alpha$ for the fraction of cycles that actually toggle), the switching power is

$$P_{dyn} = \alpha\, C\, V_{DD}^2\, f.$$

The headline relation $P = C V^2 f$ has two huge consequences. First, power scales **linearly with frequency** but **quadratically with voltage** — which is why every low-power technique reaches first for *lowering the supply* (and why the rail progression 5 V → 3.3 V → 1.8 V → below happened). Halving $V_{DD}$ cuts dynamic power to a quarter. Second, there is a smaller **short-circuit (shoot-through)** term: during the input transition both transistors are briefly on, creating a momentary rail-to-rail current spike. Fast edges minimize this; slow input edges make it worse, which is one reason you never feed a slowly-ramping signal into a logic input.

items.front: Write the CMOS dynamic (switching) power equation and name each term.
items.back: $P_{dyn}=\alpha C V_{DD}^2 f$, where alpha is the activity factor (fraction of cycles that toggle), C the switched load capacitance, V_DD the supply, and f the clock frequency.
items.hint: Charge/discharge of C every toggle.

items.front: Why is lowering the supply voltage the most powerful lever for cutting CMOS dynamic power?
items.back: Dynamic power goes as V_DD squared, so it depends quadratically on supply but only linearly on frequency. Halving V_DD cuts dynamic power to one quarter, which is why supply rails kept dropping (5 V -> 3.3 V -> 1.8 V -> lower).
items.hint: Which variable is squared in P = CV^2 f?

items.front: Name the sources of static (leakage) power in CMOS and say when they matter.
items.back: Subthreshold leakage, gate-oxide tunneling, and junction leakage. Negligible in older large-geometry logic, but a first-order problem in deep-submicron nodes, driving power gating and high-Vt cells.
items.hint: Current that flows even with no switching.

items.front: What is short-circuit (shoot-through) power and what makes it worse?
items.back: During an input transition, both the PMOS and NMOS are momentarily on, creating a brief rail-to-rail current spike. Slow input edges keep both devices on longer and increase this loss; fast, clean edges minimize it.
items.hint: Both transistors briefly conducting during the edge.

items.front: Of the energy C*V_DD^2 drawn from the supply to charge a load each cycle, how much is dissipated and where?
items.back: Half, (1/2)C*V_DD^2, is dissipated in the PMOS while charging; the other half is dissipated in the NMOS during the next discharge. The energy stored on C is fully dissipated over a charge/discharge pair.
items.hint: Equal split between charge and discharge phases.

items.prompt: A CMOS block dissipates 40 mW dynamic power at 100 MHz and 1.8 V. If you drop the supply to 0.9 V (same C and frequency), the dynamic power becomes about:
items.explanation: P scales as V^2. Halving V multiplies power by (0.5)^2 = 0.25, so 40 mW -> 10 mW.

items.prompt: Doubling only the clock frequency of a CMOS circuit (same V_DD, C, activity) changes dynamic power by a factor of:
items.explanation: Dynamic power is linear in frequency (P = alpha*C*V_DD^2*f), so doubling f doubles the dynamic power.

items.prompt: Why should you avoid driving a CMOS logic input with a slowly ramping (slow rise/fall) signal?
choices: It prolongs the interval where both transistors conduct, raising short-circuit power and causing possible oscillation
items.explanation: A slow edge keeps the input near the switching threshold longer, so PMOS and NMOS conduct simultaneously, wasting short-circuit power and risking output glitching/oscillation near the trip point. Use a Schmitt-trigger input for slow signals.

items.prompt: For a 0.1 nF load switched fully at 50 MHz on a 3.3 V rail (activity factor 1), the dynamic power is closest to:

lessons.title: Logic Families, Voltage Levels & Noise Margins
lessons.content:
A logic family is a recipe for how a part defines its levels and drives loads. **TTL (74xx, 74LS)** is the legacy bipolar family: output HIGH only guaranteed to ~2.4 V, input threshold around 1.4 V, and it sinks far more than it sources. **CMOS** families dominate today: **HC** (full-CMOS thresholds, ~$V_{DD}/2$), **HCT** (TTL-compatible inputs for mixing with TTL), **AC/ACT**, and the modern low-voltage lines **LVC** and **LVT** (3.3 V/2.5 V/1.8 V operation, often 5 V-tolerant inputs, very high drive). LVC is the current go-to for fast, mixed-voltage glue logic.

Four parameters define interoperability. The **driver** guarantees a minimum HIGH output $V_{OH}$ and a maximum LOW output $V_{OL}$. The **receiver** guarantees it will read any voltage above $V_{IH}$ as HIGH and below $V_{IL}$ as LOW. Between $V_{IL}$ and $V_{IH}$ is the **forbidden zone**. The slack between what the driver promises and what the receiver demands is the **noise margin**:

$$NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}, \qquad NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}.$$

This is exactly the margin reasoning from your radar days: any noise smaller than the margin cannot flip the bit. To check if part A can drive part B directly, confirm both margins are positive at the worst-case rails and currents. A classic trap: a 3.3 V CMOS driver feeding a 5 V CMOS input with $V_{IH}=3.5$ V fails ($NM_H<0$) even though 3.3 V "looks high" — whereas the same driver into an HCT or LVC input (TTL-level $V_{IH}\approx2.0$ V) works fine. Always read the datasheet, never assume.

items.front: Define V_OH, V_OL, V_IH, and V_IL.
items.back: V_OH: minimum guaranteed HIGH output voltage of a driver. V_OL: maximum guaranteed LOW output voltage. V_IH: minimum voltage a receiver reads as HIGH. V_IL: maximum voltage a receiver reads as LOW. Outputs are referenced to the driver, inputs to the receiver.
items.hint: O = output (driver), I = input (receiver).

items.front: Write the high and low noise-margin equations and explain what they protect against.
items.back: $NM_H=V_{OH}-V_{IH}$ and $NM_L=V_{IL}-V_{OL}$. They are the slack between what the driver guarantees and what the receiver requires; any noise smaller than the margin cannot corrupt the logic level.
items.hint: Difference between driver promise and receiver demand.

items.front: What is the 'forbidden zone' between V_IL and V_IH?
items.back: The input voltage band where the receiver does not guarantee a defined HIGH or LOW. A static signal parked here is illegal; it may be read inconsistently and (in CMOS) cause shoot-through. Signals must transit it quickly.
items.hint: Region of undefined logic level.

items.front: What distinguishes 74HC from 74HCT inputs, and why does HCT exist?
items.back: HC uses full-CMOS input thresholds near V_DD/2. HCT uses TTL-compatible input thresholds (V_IH ~ 2.0 V), so it can be driven directly by older TTL or by 3.3 V parts. HCT exists to bridge CMOS to TTL logic levels.
items.hint: The 'T' means TTL-compatible inputs.

items.front: Where do LVC and LVT families fit in the modern landscape?
items.back: They are low-voltage CMOS families running at 3.3/2.5/1.8 V, often with 5 V-tolerant inputs and very high drive strength. LVC is the common choice for fast, mixed-voltage bus and glue logic; LVT is a bipolar-CMOS variant for heavy bus drive.
items.hint: Low-voltage, high-drive, mixed-rail glue logic.

items.prompt: A driver guarantees V_OH = 2.4 V. The receiver requires V_IH = 2.0 V. The high-side noise margin NM_H is:
items.explanation: NM_H = V_OH - V_IH = 2.4 - 2.0 = 0.4 V. It is positive, so a HIGH is reliably recognized with 0.4 V of noise immunity.

items.prompt: A 3.3 V CMOS part (V_OH = 3.0 V) drives a 5 V CMOS input with V_IH = 3.5 V. Does the HIGH level work, and why?
choices: No, because NM_H = 3.0 - 3.5 = -0.5 V, so the HIGH is not guaranteed
No, because the currents are too high
items.explanation: Full-CMOS 5 V inputs need ~3.5 V (0.7*V_DD) to read HIGH. A 3.0 V drive gives a negative high-side margin, so it can be misread. A level shifter or a TTL-level (HCT/LVC) input is required.

items.prompt: Which family pairing lets a 3.3 V CMOS output reliably drive a 5 V-powered receiver's HIGH level without a level shifter?
items.explanation: An HCT input only needs ~2.0 V to register HIGH, so a 3.3 V (3.0 V V_OH) drive clears it with margin. A plain HC input at V_DD/2 ~ 2.5 V or 0.7*V_DD would not be reliable.

items.prompt: Compared to CMOS, classic TTL (74LS) outputs characteristically:
choices: Source far more current than they sink and swing rail-to-rail
Sink more current than they source and do not reach V_CC on a HIGH
items.explanation: TTL totem-pole outputs sink well but source weakly, and a HIGH is only guaranteed to about 2.4 V, not the full rail. This asymmetry (and the low V_OH) is central to TTL-vs-CMOS interfacing.

items.front: Logic-level bands and noise margins (driver vs. receiver)
items.back: The driver guarantees outputs above V_OH (HIGH) or below V_OL (LOW). The receiver reads above V_IH as HIGH, below V_IL as LOW, with a forbidden zone between. The vertical gaps NM_H = V_OH - V_IH and NM_L = V_IL - V_OL are the noise immunity.

lessons.title: Fan-Out, Open-Drain, Level Shifting & Decoupling
lessons.content:
**Fan-out** is how many inputs one output can drive. For CMOS the DC fan-out is enormous (inputs are capacitive, near-zero DC load), so the real limit is **dynamic**: each driven input adds capacitance, slowing edges and raising $P=CV^2f$. For TTL, fan-out is a hard DC count set by the ratio of output sink/source current to input current. Either way, exceeding fan-out degrades levels and timing.

A **floating CMOS input is a hazard**: with no defined level it drifts near the threshold, drawing shoot-through current and oscillating. Every unused input gets a **pull-up or pull-down** resistor (typically 10 kΩ); active inputs that can tristate need one too.

**Open-drain (open-collector)** outputs omit the pull-up transistor: the device can only pull LOW or release to Hi-Z. An external pull-up defines the HIGH. Tie several together and you get **wired-AND** — the line is HIGH only if *every* device releases it; any one pulling LOW wins. This is the electrical basis of **I²C** and of interrupt/handshake lines, and it cleanly bridges voltage domains (the pull-up can go to the higher rail).

**Level shifting** between rails is mandatory when noise margins go negative. Options: open-drain + pull-up to the target rail (bidirectional, slow), dedicated level-translator ICs (fast, directional or auto-sensing), or a single N-MOSFET for bidirectional I²C-style lines. Finally, **decoupling**: every IC needs a ~100 nF ceramic cap right at its power pins to supply the transient charge for switching (that $C V_{DD}^2 f$ current comes from somewhere), keeping the local rail stiff and stopping switching noise from coupling into neighbors — your analog grounding instincts apply directly.

items.front: What limits fan-out for CMOS vs. TTL?
items.back: CMOS inputs draw negligible DC current, so DC fan-out is huge; the real limit is dynamic (added capacitance slows edges and raises C*V^2*f). TTL fan-out is a hard DC count set by output drive current divided by input current.
items.hint: Capacitive/dynamic for CMOS, DC current for TTL.

items.front: Why is a floating CMOS input dangerous, and what is the fix?
items.back: With no driven level it drifts near the switching threshold, causing shoot-through current, oscillation, and undefined logic. Fix: tie every unused or tristatable input to a defined level with a pull-up or pull-down resistor (~10 kohm).
items.hint: Undefined level parks the gate in its linear region.

items.front: How does an open-drain output work and what produces the HIGH level?
items.back: It has only the NMOS pull-down: it can drive LOW or go Hi-Z (released), but it cannot drive HIGH itself. An external pull-up resistor to the chosen rail produces the HIGH when the output is released.
items.hint: No internal pull-up; needs an external resistor.

items.front: Explain wired-AND on an open-drain bus and name a protocol that uses it.
items.back: Multiple open-drain outputs share one pulled-up line. It is HIGH only if all devices release it; any single device pulling LOW forces the whole line LOW (logical AND). This is the basis of I2C (SDA/SCL) and shared interrupt/handshake lines.
items.hint: Any one driver can pull the shared line low.

items.front: Why does every IC get a ~100 nF decoupling capacitor at its power pins?
items.back: Switching draws transient charge (the C*V_DD^2*f current). A local ceramic cap supplies that charge instantly, keeping the local rail stiff and preventing switching noise from propagating along the power distribution into neighboring parts and analog references.
items.hint: Local charge reservoir for switching transients.

items.prompt: On a shared open-drain bus with pull-up, if one device drives LOW while all others are released, the bus line is:
items.explanation: Open-drain gives wired-AND behavior: any device pulling LOW dominates, so the line is LOW. It is HIGH only when every device releases and the pull-up takes over.

items.prompt: When is level shifting between two logic domains mandatory?
choices: Only when the supply currents differ
When a noise margin (NM_H or NM_L) would be negative for a direct connection
items.explanation: If the driver's guaranteed output level fails to clear the receiver's required input threshold (negative noise margin), the bit can be misread; a level translator restores positive margins.

items.prompt: A common single-device technique for bidirectional level shifting on an I2C-style open-drain line uses:
choices: An N-channel MOSFET with pull-ups on both sides
items.explanation: The classic Philips/NXP circuit uses one N-MOSFET with a pull-up to each rail; it passes lows in both directions and lets each side's pull-up set its HIGH, translating between the two open-drain domains bidirectionally.

items.prompt: Adding many CMOS loads to one output most directly degrades the design by:
choices: Drawing large DC current that overheats the driver
Increasing total load capacitance, slowing edges and raising dynamic power
Raising the threshold voltage of the receivers
items.explanation: CMOS inputs are essentially capacitive, so extra loads add capacitance. That slows rise/fall times (timing risk) and increases C*V^2*f dynamic power, which is the practical fan-out limit.

items.prompt: An advantage of an open-drain output beyond wired-AND is that it can:
choices: Interface to a higher-voltage rail simply by pulling its external resistor up to that rail
Eliminate the need for any decoupling
Switch faster than a push-pull output for the same load
items.explanation: Because the HIGH is set by the external pull-up, the resistor can go to a different (often higher) rail, giving simple unidirectional level translation. The tradeoff is slower rising edges (RC-limited) versus a push-pull output.

lessons.title: Selectors: Multiplexers, Demuxes, Decoders & Encoders
lessons.content:
Combinational logic is memoryless: outputs are a pure function of present inputs. The design flow is always **spec → truth table → minimized logic → schematic**. This lesson covers the routing/selection primitives that dominate real datapaths.

A **multiplexer (mux)** selects one of $2^n$ data inputs onto a single output using $n$ select lines. The 2:1 mux is the atom:

$$Y = \overline{S}\,I_0 + S\,I_1$$

The key insight for a software dev: **every `if`/`case`/ternary in HDL synthesizes to a mux.** Muxes are also *universal* — a $2^n$:1 mux with the variables on its select lines implements **any** $n$-variable function by hardwiring each data input to the corresponding truth-table row (0 or 1). You can even do an $n$-variable function with a $2^{n-1}$:1 mux by feeding the last variable (or its complement, or 0/1) into the data inputs (Shannon expansion).

A **demultiplexer** is the inverse: it routes one input to one of $2^n$ outputs. A **decoder** (e.g. 3:8) asserts exactly one of $2^n$ outputs for each input code — it generates all minterms, so it's a natural SOP engine and the basis of address/chip-select decoding.

An **encoder** does the reverse (one-hot → binary) but breaks if two inputs are active. A **priority encoder** fixes this by outputting the code of the *highest-priority* asserted input plus a "valid" bit — exactly what an interrupt controller does. As an ex-radar tech: think of a decoder as range-gate selection and a priority encoder as picking the strongest return.

items.front: 2:1 multiplexer symbol and equation
items.back: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/GoldCoin1987/Learning-App/main/packs/digital-electronics/images/combinational-blocks-2.svg

items.front: Write the Boolean equation for a 2:1 multiplexer with inputs $I_0$, $I_1$ and select $S$.
items.back: $Y = \overline{S}\,I_0 + S\,I_1$. When $S=0$ the output follows $I_0$; when $S=1$ it follows $I_1$.
items.hint: Select line and its complement gate the two data inputs.

items.front: Why is an $n$-select multiplexer called a universal logic element?
items.back: A $2^n$:1 mux with the input variables driving its select lines can realize ANY $n$-variable function: tie each data input to that minterm's truth-table value (0 or 1). Each select code picks the right row.
items.hint: Think about what the data inputs represent relative to a truth table.

items.front: What does an $n$-to-$2^n$ decoder output, and why is it a natural SOP generator?
items.back: It asserts exactly one of its $2^n$ outputs — the one matching the input code. Each output line is one minterm, so ORing selected outputs builds any sum-of-products function directly.
items.hint: One-hot output, one line per input combination.

items.front: How does a priority encoder differ from a plain encoder?
items.back: A plain encoder produces wrong output if more than one input is active. A priority encoder outputs the binary code of the HIGHEST-priority active input and adds a 'valid'/'any' output. Used in interrupt controllers.
items.hint: What happens when two inputs are high at once?

items.front: Contrast a demultiplexer with a decoder.
items.back: A demux routes a single data input to one of $2^n$ outputs chosen by the select lines. A decoder with an enable input IS a demux — feed data into the enable and the address into the select lines.
items.hint: They share hardware; the difference is which line carries 'data'.

items.prompt: You want to implement a 4-variable function using a single 8:1 mux (3 select lines). What goes on the data inputs?
choices: The fourth variable, its complement, 0, or 1 as determined by Shannon expansion
All four variables on the data inputs and constants on select
It is impossible; you need a 16:1 mux
items.explanation: With 3 variables on the select lines, the residue function of the 4th variable on each data line is one of {0, 1, $x_4$, $\overline{x_4}$}. This Shannon-expansion trick halves the needed mux size.

items.prompt: In HDL, which construct most directly synthesizes into a multiplexer?
choices: A `case` statement selecting an output by a control signal
items.explanation: `if`/`case`/ternary expressions that choose among values based on a control signal map directly to muxes — the single most common structure inferred by synthesis. A clocked block infers flip-flops instead.

items.prompt: An 8-to-3 priority encoder has inputs $D_5$ and $D_2$ both asserted (priority increases with index). What is the 3-bit output?
items.explanation: The highest-index active input wins. $D_5$ outranks $D_2$, so the output is binary 101 = 5, with the valid bit set.

lessons.title: Comparators, Parity & Code Converters
lessons.content:
Beyond routing, combinational logic answers questions about data. A **comparator** evaluates two operands. Equality is the simplest: bitwise XNOR each pair and AND the results.

$$\text{EQ} = \prod_{i=0}^{n-1}\overline{(A_i \oplus B_i)}$$

**Magnitude** comparison ($A>B$, $A=B$, $A<B$) is built MSB-first: compare the top bits; only descend to lower bits where the higher bits are equal. Cascadable comparators (e.g. 74x85) chain this across bytes.

**Parity** is pure XOR. An even-parity generator outputs $P = D_0 \oplus D_1 \oplus \cdots \oplus D_{n-1}$, appended so the total number of 1s is even. A checker XORs all received bits (including the parity bit); a nonzero result flags an odd number of bit errors. As a radar tech you met this on telemetry links — single-bit error *detection*, no correction, and it misses even-count errors. Modern links extend the idea to CRC (an XOR/shift structure) and Hamming/ECC codes.

**Code converters** are just functions tabulated and minimized. Two recur constantly:
- **Binary ↔ Gray**: to Gray, $G_i = B_{i+1} \oplus B_i$ with $G_{\text{MSB}} = B_{\text{MSB}}$ (one XOR per bit). Gray's single-bit-change property prevents decoding glitches in encoders and is essential for clock-domain crossing.
- **Binary → BCD** (e.g. double-dabble) and the **7-segment decoder**, a classic 4-input/7-output truth-table-to-logic exercise that drives displays.

All of these are memoryless functions: derive the truth table, minimize (next lesson's K-maps help), done.

items.front: How do you build an $n$-bit equality comparator from gates?
items.back: XNOR each bit pair ($A_i \odot B_i$ is true when bits match), then AND all the XNOR outputs: $\text{EQ} = \prod_i \overline{(A_i \oplus B_i)}$. Any mismatch forces EQ low.
items.hint: XNOR detects 'bits are equal'.

items.front: What ordering does a magnitude comparator use to decide $A>B$?
items.back: MSB-first. Compare the most significant bits; the operand with a 1 where the other has 0 (at the highest differing position) is larger. Lower bits only matter where all higher bits are equal.
items.hint: Same way you compare multi-digit decimal numbers.

items.front: Write the even-parity bit for data $D_0 \ldots D_{n-1}$ and state what a checker computes.
items.back: $P = D_0 \oplus D_1 \oplus \cdots \oplus D_{n-1}$ (XOR of all data bits). The checker XORs all received bits including $P$; a result of 1 means an odd number of bits flipped (error detected).
items.hint: Parity is just a big XOR tree.

items.front: What is the fundamental limitation of single-bit parity?
items.back: It detects only an ODD number of bit errors and cannot correct any. Two flipped bits (or any even count) pass undetected. Stronger schemes (CRC, Hamming/ECC) are needed for detection of bursts or for correction.
items.hint: Think about what happens with two simultaneous errors.

items.front: Give the binary-to-Gray conversion formula and why Gray code matters here.
items.back: $G_{\text{MSB}} = B_{\text{MSB}}$, and $G_i = B_{i+1} \oplus B_i$ for lower bits. Successive Gray codes differ by exactly one bit, eliminating decode glitches in shaft/position encoders and enabling safe clock-domain crossing.
items.hint: One XOR per bit, top bit unchanged.

items.prompt: Convert binary 1011 to Gray code.
A 7-segment decoder is best characterized as which kind of circuit?
choices: A sequential circuit requiring a clock
A combinational truth-table mapping 4 input bits to 7 output segments
items.explanation: It is purely combinational: 4 input lines (a BCD/hex digit) map to 7 segment-drive outputs via a fixed truth table that you minimize per segment. No state, no clock.

items.prompt: A byte protected by even parity arrives with two bits flipped during transmission. What does the parity checker report?
choices: It flags an error and identifies the bad bits
No error — the errors go undetected
It always reports a framing error
items.explanation: Two flips keep the total number of 1s the same parity, so the checker computes 0 (no error). Even-count errors are invisible to single-bit parity — its core weakness.

lessons.title: Adders: Half, Full, Ripple-Carry & Carry-Lookahead
lessons.content:
Arithmetic is the heart of the datapath. A **half-adder** adds two bits:

$$S = A \oplus B, \qquad C_{\text{out}} = A \cdot B$$

It can't accept a carry-in, so it's only useful for the LSB. A **full-adder** adds three bits ($A$, $B$, $C_{\text{in}}$):

$$S = A \oplus B \oplus C_{\text{in}}, \qquad C_{\text{out}} = AB + C_{\text{in}}(A \oplus B)$$

Chain $n$ full-adders, carry-out to carry-in, and you get a **ripple-carry adder (RCA)**. It's tiny and regular, but slow: the carry must propagate through every stage, so worst-case delay grows **linearly**, $t \approx n \cdot t_{\text{FA}}$ — the classic critical path.

**Carry-lookahead (CLA)** breaks this. Define per-bit **generate** and **propagate**:

$$G_i = A_i B_i, \qquad P_i = A_i \oplus B_i$$

Then $C_{i+1} = G_i + P_i C_i$, which unrolls into a flat expression — e.g.

$$C_1 = G_0 + P_0 C_0, \quad C_2 = G_1 + P_1 G_0 + P_1 P_0 C_0$$

All carries compute in parallel from $C_0$, giving roughly **logarithmic** delay at the cost of more gates and fan-in. **Carry-select** adders take a different tack: compute each block twice (assuming carry-in 0 and 1) and mux the right answer once the real carry arrives — trading area for speed. **Subtraction** reuses the adder: $A - B = A + \overline{B} + 1$, forcing $C_0 = 1$ and inverting $B$ (two's complement). Multipliers are arrays of adders (array/Wallace-tree); these become the MAC units that dominate FPGA DSP and radar pulse compression.

items.front: Full-adder gate diagram
items.back: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/GoldCoin1987/Learning-App/main/packs/digital-electronics/images/combinational-blocks-1.svg

items.front: Give the sum and carry equations for a half-adder and state its limitation.
items.back: $S = A \oplus B$, $C_{\text{out}} = A \cdot B$. Limitation: it has no carry-in, so it can only be used at the least-significant bit position of a multi-bit adder.
items.hint: XOR for sum, AND for carry; what's missing as an input?

items.front: Write the full-adder sum and carry-out equations.
items.back: $S = A \oplus B \oplus C_{\text{in}}$ and $C_{\text{out}} = AB + C_{\text{in}}(A \oplus B)$ (equivalently $AB + AC_{\text{in}} + BC_{\text{in}}$, the majority function).
items.hint: Sum is three-way XOR; carry is majority-of-three.

items.front: Why is a ripple-carry adder slow, and how does its delay scale with width $n$?
items.back: Each stage's carry-out depends on the previous stage's carry, so the carry ripples serially through all stages. Worst-case delay is linear: $t \approx n \cdot t_{\text{FA}}$. This carry chain is the critical path.
items.hint: Follow the carry from LSB to MSB.

items.front: Define the generate and propagate signals used in carry-lookahead.
items.back: $G_i = A_i B_i$ (the bit generates a carry regardless of carry-in) and $P_i = A_i \oplus B_i$ (the bit propagates an incoming carry). Then $C_{i+1} = G_i + P_i C_i$.
items.hint: One ANDs the inputs, the other XORs them.

items.front: How is two's-complement subtraction $A-B$ done with an adder?
items.back: Compute $A + \overline{B} + 1$: invert every bit of $B$ and force the carry-in $C_0 = 1$. A single XOR row controlled by a 'sub' line lets one adder do both add and subtract.
items.hint: Negation = invert + add one.

items.prompt: Compared to ripple-carry, carry-lookahead primarily improves which metric, and at what cost?
choices: Reduces gate count at the cost of speed
Reduces carry-propagation delay (toward logarithmic) at the cost of more gates and higher fan-in
items.explanation: CLA computes all carries in parallel from the generate/propagate terms, cutting the linear ripple delay toward logarithmic. The price is more logic and larger fan-in, which is why deep CLAs are built hierarchically in blocks.

items.prompt: Using $C_{i+1}=G_i+P_iC_i$, which expression correctly expands $C_2$ in terms of $C_0$?
items.explanation: Substitute $C_1 = G_0 + P_0 C_0$ into $C_2 = G_1 + P_1 C_1$: $C_2 = G_1 + P_1(G_0 + P_0 C_0) = G_1 + P_1 G_0 + P_1 P_0 C_0$. This flat form lets $C_2$ form without waiting on $C_1$.

items.prompt: How does a carry-select adder gain speed?
choices: It computes each block twice (for carry-in 0 and 1) and muxes the correct result once the real carry arrives
It removes the carry chain entirely
It uses sequential logic clocked at a higher frequency
items.explanation: Each block is duplicated and precomputes both possible carry-in cases in parallel; a fast mux then selects the right sum when the incoming carry is known. It trades area (two adders per block plus a mux) for reduced latency.

lessons.title: The ALU, Flags & Combinational Hazards
lessons.content:
An **ALU** fuses the building blocks into one programmable datapath element: a **function-select** input picks among add, subtract, AND, OR, XOR, and shift. A clean structure is a 1-bit **ALU slice** — a full-adder plus logic gates plus an output mux — replicated and chained via the carry to form an $n$-bit ALU. This is the literal heart of every CPU you'll meet in the embedded module.

The ALU also produces **condition flags**:
- **Z (Zero):** result is all zeros, $Z = \overline{R_{n-1} + \cdots + R_0}$ (NOR of result bits).
- **N (Negative):** sign bit of the result, $N = R_{n-1}$ (two's-complement MSB).
- **C (Carry):** carry-out of the MSB — the *unsigned* overflow / borrow indicator.
- **V (Overflow):** *signed* overflow, $V = C_{n-1} \oplus C_{n}$ (carry into vs out of the MSB differ), or equivalently when two like-signed operands produce an opposite-signed result.

The classic rust-knocker: **C and V are different.** $C$ matters for unsigned math; $V$ matters for signed. Mixing them corrupts comparisons and arithmetic.

**Hazards** are transient wrong outputs (glitches) from unequal path delays, even when steady-state logic is correct. A **static-1 hazard**: an output that should stay 1 momentarily dips to 0 (typical of SOP when moving between adjacent K-map terms not covered by a common group). A **static-0 hazard** is the dual. A **dynamic hazard** is multiple transitions on a single intended change, from reconvergent multi-level paths. The fix for a static hazard is to add the **redundant (consensus) product term** that bridges the adjacent groups on the K-map — it costs a gate but holds the output steady. Glitches are harmless into synchronous (clocked) logic if they settle before setup, but deadly on asynchronous control lines, clocks, or resets.

items.front: Describe the structure of a 1-bit ALU slice and how an $n$-bit ALU is built from it.
items.back: A slice = a full-adder (for add/sub) + AND/OR/XOR gates, with an output mux selected by the function-select lines. Stack $n$ slices, chaining carry-out to carry-in, to form the $n$-bit ALU.
items.hint: Arithmetic unit + logic unit + a selecting mux, replicated bitwise.

items.front: How is the Zero (Z) flag computed from an $n$-bit result?
items.back: $Z = \overline{R_{n-1} + R_{n-2} + \cdots + R_0}$ — the NOR of all result bits. Z is 1 only when every result bit is 0.
items.hint: All bits zero → NOR them.

items.front: Distinguish the Carry (C) flag from the Overflow (V) flag.
items.back: C is the carry-out of the MSB — it signals UNSIGNED overflow/borrow. V signals SIGNED (two's-complement) overflow: $V = C_{\text{in,MSB}} \oplus C_{\text{out,MSB}}$. They are independent; use C for unsigned math, V for signed.
items.hint: One is for unsigned, the other for two's-complement.

items.front: Give two equivalent ways to detect signed overflow (V) in an adder.
items.back: (1) XOR the carry into the MSB with the carry out of the MSB: $V = C_{n-1} \oplus C_n$. (2) Two operands of the SAME sign yield a result of the OPPOSITE sign. Both detect that the true result fell outside the representable signed range.
items.hint: Carry-in vs carry-out of the top bit; or sign-pattern of operands vs result.

items.front: What is a static-1 hazard and how is it eliminated?
items.back: A momentary 0-glitch on an output that should remain 1, caused by unequal delays when the input moves between two adjacent minterms covered by different SOP product terms. Fix: add the redundant (consensus) product term that overlaps both groups on the K-map.
items.hint: Glitch while output should stay high; bridge the K-map groups.

items.front: What is a dynamic hazard, and when do hazards actually matter?
items.back: A dynamic hazard is multiple transitions (e.g. 1→0→1→0) on an output that should change just once, arising from reconvergent multi-level paths with differing delays. Hazards are harmless into clocked logic if settled before setup, but dangerous on async lines: clocks, resets, latch enables.
items.hint: More than one transition on a single intended change.

items.prompt: Adding two 8-bit two's-complement numbers, 0x70 + 0x10, gives 0x80. Which flags are set?
choices: C=0, V=1, N=1 (signed overflow; result looks negative)
items.explanation: 0x70 (+112) + 0x10 (+16) = 0x80, which as signed is −128 — two positives produced a negative, so V=1 and N=1. There is no carry out of bit 7, so C=0. This is exactly the C≠V trap.

items.prompt: A two-level SOP circuit shows a static-1 hazard between two adjacent K-map cells. What is the standard fix?
choices: Add a redundant consensus product term covering both adjacent groups
Convert the circuit to POS form
items.explanation: Adding the redundant (consensus) term creates an always-covering group spanning the transition, so the output never drops while inputs change between the two cells. It costs an extra gate but is the canonical hazard cure. Clock speed is irrelevant to a combinational glitch.

items.prompt: When are combinational glitches genuinely dangerous rather than harmless?
choices: Always — every glitch corrupts the design
When they feed asynchronous inputs like clocks, resets, or latch enables
Only when the supply voltage is below 3.3 V
items.explanation: Into a clocked flip-flop, a glitch that settles before the setup window is sampled away harmlessly. But a glitch on a clock, asynchronous reset/preset, or latch enable can trigger a false event — that's where hazard-free covering matters.

lessons.title: The K-Map as a Geometry of Adjacency
lessons.content:
A Karnaugh map is just a truth table re-drawn so that **physically adjacent cells differ in exactly one input variable**. That single-variable-change property is the whole trick, and it comes from labelling the axes in **Gray code** ($00, 01, 11, 10$) instead of straight binary ($00, 01, 10, 11$). Walk left-to-right across a row and only one bit flips per step; the same holds top-to-bottom and, crucially, across the wrap-around edges (the map is a torus).

Why does this matter? Boolean algebra's **combining theorem** says

$$X\cdot Y + X\cdot \overline{Y} = X.$$

Two minterms that differ in one variable collapse, eliminating that variable. On the map those two minterms are *adjacent cells*, so "draw a loop around adjacent 1s" is the visual form of applying that theorem. A 2-variable map is a $2\times 2$ grid (4 cells), a 3-variable map is $2\times 4$ (8 cells), and a 4-variable map is $4\times 4$ (16 cells), one cell per minterm.

Each cell holds the function's output for that input combination. You fill it directly from the truth table or from the canonical SOP minterm list. As an ex-radar tech you already read state tables fluently; the K-map is the same data with the rows shuffled so that the *geometry encodes the algebra*. Get the Gray ordering and the cell-to-minterm mapping reflexive first — every later step (grouping, prime implicants, don't-cares) rides on top of this adjacency structure.

items.front: Why are K-map axes labelled in Gray code instead of straight binary?
items.back: So that any two physically adjacent cells differ in exactly one input variable. That single-bit change is what lets the combining theorem X·Y + X·Y' = X apply to neighboring cells.
items.hint: Think about what must be true of two cells you want to merge into one term.

items.front: How many cells does an n-variable K-map have, and what does each cell represent?
items.back: 2^n cells, one per minterm (one row of the truth table). Each cell holds the function's output for that specific input combination.

items.front: State the combining theorem that K-map grouping visualizes.
items.back: X·Y + X·Y' = X. Two product terms identical except for one variable (which appears true in one and complemented in the other) merge, eliminating that variable.

items.front: What is 'wrap-around adjacency' on a K-map?
items.back: The left and right edges are adjacent, and the top and bottom edges are adjacent (the map behaves like a torus). The four corners of a 4-variable map, for example, are all mutually adjacent.
items.hint: The map has no real borders for adjacency purposes.

items.front: Write the Gray-code ordering used along a 4-cell K-map axis.
items.back: 00, 01, 11, 10 — each step changes one bit, including the wrap from 10 back to 00.

items.prompt: On a 4-variable K-map, why are minterms m0 (0000) and m8 (1000) considered adjacent even though they sit in different rows?
choices: They differ in exactly one variable (A), and Gray-code row ordering places them in adjacent (wrap-around) positions
They are both in column 00 so any two cells in a column are adjacent regardless of bits
They are never adjacent; 0000 and 1000 cannot combine
items.explanation: 0000 and 1000 differ only in A. With Gray-coded AB rows (00,01,11,10), AB=00 and AB=10 are adjacent via the top/bottom wrap, so the cells touch and combine to eliminate A.

items.prompt: A 3-variable K-map is most commonly drawn as which grid shape?
choices: 1 x 8 (8 cells in a single row)
items.explanation: Three variables give 2^3 = 8 minterms, conventionally arranged as a 2-row by 4-column grid (one variable on rows, two Gray-coded on columns).

items.prompt: If a K-map's columns were labelled 00, 01, 10, 11 (straight binary) instead of Gray code, what breaks?
choices: Horizontally adjacent cells would sometimes differ in two variables, so visual grouping would no longer correspond to the combining theorem
Nothing — the labelling is purely cosmetic
The map would need twice as many cells
The function's truth table would change
items.explanation: Between columns 01 and 10, two bits change. Adjacency would no longer guarantee a single-variable difference, so looping neighbors would not legally merge terms. Gray ordering is what makes geometric adjacency equal logical adjacency.

items.prompt: You fill a K-map directly from a function's canonical SOP form. Where do the 1s go?
choices: In the cells whose minterm numbers appear in the SOP minterm list
In every cell, then you erase the maxterms
Only in the four corner cells
In the cells whose indices are prime numbers
items.explanation: Canonical SOP is a list of minterms where the function equals 1. Each listed minterm number maps to one cell; place a 1 there and 0 elsewhere.

lessons.title: Grouping, Prime Implicants & SOP/POS Minimization
lessons.content:
Minimization is the art of covering every 1 with the **fewest, largest legal groups**. The rules: a group must be a rectangle of $2^k$ cells ($1, 2, 4, 8, 16$), may wrap around edges, and must contain only 1s (for SOP). A group of $2^k$ cells in an $n$-variable map eliminates $k$ variables, leaving an $(n-k)$-literal product term — so **bigger groups are cheaper**. Always grab the largest group each 1 can belong to.

Vocabulary that earns its keep when you read synthesis reports:

- An **implicant** is any legal group (any product term that implies the function).
- A **prime implicant (PI)** is a group that cannot be enlarged — no bigger legal rectangle contains it.
- An **essential prime implicant (EPI)** is a PI that is the *only* PI covering some particular 1. EPIs must appear in any minimal cover.

The algorithm: list all PIs, select every EPI, then cover the remaining 1s with as few additional PIs as possible. See the diagram of a 4-cell group reducing to $B\cdot D$.

**SOP vs POS** is a duality. To get a minimal product-of-sums, group the **0s** instead of the 1s, read each group as a sum term using complemented logic, and combine with AND. Equivalently, by De Morgan,

$$\overline{F} = \sum m(\text{the 0 cells}), \quad F = \overline{\overline{F}}.$$

Choose whichever has fewer/larger groups: a function dense with 1s often yields a cheaper POS. The minimal SOP and minimal POS describe the *same* function but can differ in gate count, delay, and which hazards appear.

items.front: Define a prime implicant (PI).
