items.back: A legal group (rectangle of 2^k cells of 1s) that cannot be enlarged into any bigger legal group. It is a maximal implicant of the function.

items.front: Define an essential prime implicant (EPI) and state why it matters.
items.back: An EPI is a prime implicant that is the only PI covering at least one specific 1-cell. Because nothing else can cover that minterm, every minimal cover must include all EPIs.
items.hint: Look for a 1 that belongs to exactly one prime implicant.

items.front: How many variables does a group of 2^k cells eliminate, and how many literals remain in an n-variable map?
items.back: It eliminates k variables, leaving an (n - k)-literal product term. Larger groups => fewer literals => cheaper logic.

items.front: How do you obtain a minimal POS expression from a K-map?
items.back: Group the 0s (not the 1s) into the largest legal groups, read each group as a sum term (a variable is complemented if it is 1 across the group), and AND the sum terms together. This is the De Morgan dual of SOP grouping.

items.front: What is the recommended order of operations once all prime implicants are listed?
items.back: 1) Select all essential prime implicants. 2) Remove the 1s they cover. 3) Cover any remaining 1s with the fewest additional prime implicants (the covering problem).

items.prompt: On a 4-variable map, a group circles cells {5, 7, 13, 15}. What product term does it represent?
choices: A' AND B AND C AND D
items.explanation: Across those four cells B=1 and D=1 are constant while A and C take all combinations. The group of 4 eliminates the two varying variables (A, C), leaving B·D.

items.prompt: Which statement about a minimal cover is correct?
choices: It must contain every essential prime implicant, plus the fewest non-essential PIs needed to cover the rest
It must contain every prime implicant of the function
It may never contain a non-essential prime implicant
It is always unique for any function
items.explanation: EPIs are mandatory. Remaining 1s are covered by selecting as few additional (non-essential) PIs as possible. Including all PIs would be redundant, and minimal covers are not always unique.

items.prompt: A function has 1s in 13 of a 4-variable map's 16 cells. Why might POS be cheaper than SOP here?
choices: Only 3 cells are 0, so grouping the sparse 0s likely yields fewer and larger groups than covering 13 scattered 1s
POS is always cheaper than SOP regardless of the function
SOP cannot represent functions with more than 8 ones
POS uses Gray code while SOP does not
items.explanation: Minimization cost scales with the number and size of groups needed. With few 0s, grouping the 0s (POS) tends to need fewer terms than covering many 1s (SOP). Always check both.

items.prompt: A single 1-cell can be grouped with no neighbor (all four neighbors are 0). What does this tell you?
choices: That isolated cell is itself an essential prime implicant and forces a full n-literal product term
The cell can be ignored to save logic
It means the function is a tautology
items.explanation: A lone 1 forms a group of size 1 (a 2^0 group), is the only PI covering itself (hence essential), and contributes an unreduced n-literal minterm to the cover.

lessons.title: Don't-Cares, Quine-McCluskey & Hazard Removal
lessons.content:
**Don't-cares** ($d$ or $X$) are input combinations that never occur or whose output you don't care about (e.g., the invalid codes $1010$-$1111$ in a BCD digit). Treat each $X$ as a free $1$ *only when it enlarges a group*, and as $0$ otherwise. Never cover an $X$ that buys you nothing — it is never required on its own. Exploited well, don't-cares routinely halve literal counts (see the wrap-around corner group in the second diagram).

K-maps run out of steam past 4-5 variables (a 5-var map is two stacked 4-var maps; 6-var is four). The **Quine-McCluskey** tabular method is the algorithmic generalization that gives the *same* prime implicants without geometry: list minterms grouped by bit-count, repeatedly combine terms differing in one bit (marking $-$ for the eliminated variable), and the uncombinable terms are the prime implicants. A **PI chart** then selects EPIs and a minimal cover. It is exact but exponential; real synthesizers use heuristic minimizers like **Espresso**, which trade guaranteed minimality for speed on hundreds of variables.

**Hazard removal:** a minimal cover can still **glitch**. A static-1 hazard occurs when an input change moves between two adjacent 1-cells that lie in *different* groups — momentarily, neither product term holds and the output dips. The fix is to add a **redundant (consensus) product term** that covers the overlap, bridging the two groups. The consensus of $A\cdot C$ and $\overline{A}\cdot B$ is $B\cdot C$:

$$A\cdot C + \overline{A}\cdot B + B\cdot C.$$

The extra term costs a gate but guarantees a glitch-free output — essential for asynchronous logic and any signal feeding an edge-sensitive input.

items.front: How should a don't-care (X) cell be treated during K-map grouping?
items.back: As a free 1 only when it lets you form a larger group (eliminating more variables); otherwise as a 0. Never form a group whose sole purpose is to cover an X — don't-cares are optional, never required.
items.hint: It is a wildcard, not an obligation.

items.front: Summarize the Quine-McCluskey procedure at a conceptual level.
items.back: Group minterms by number of 1-bits, repeatedly combine pairs that differ in exactly one bit (marking the dropped bit with a dash), and collect terms that can no longer combine — those are the prime implicants. A PI/coverage chart then picks EPIs and a minimal cover.

items.front: What does Espresso do that Quine-McCluskey does not, and what is the trade-off?
items.back: Espresso is a heuristic minimizer used inside real synthesis tools; it scales to hundreds of variables quickly but does not guarantee an absolute minimum. Quine-McCluskey is exact but exponential and impractical for large functions.

items.front: What is a static-1 hazard and what causes it on a K-map?
items.back: A momentary 0-glitch in an output that should stay 1, occurring when a single input change moves between two adjacent 1-cells that belong to different product-term groups — neither term covers the transition instant.
items.hint: Think about two loops that touch but do not overlap.

items.front: How do you remove a static-1 hazard, and what is the cost?
items.back: Add a redundant consensus product term that overlaps both groups straddling the hazard transition, so coverage is continuous through the input change. Cost: one extra gate; the term is logically redundant but eliminates the glitch.

items.front: Give the consensus term of A·C and A'·B.
items.back: B·C. The consensus theorem: A·C + A'·B = A·C + A'·B + B·C, where the added B·C is the redundant term used for hazard cover.

items.prompt: A BCD-input function leaves codes 1010 through 1111 unused. The minimal use of these as don't-cares is to:
choices: Mark them X and include any that enlarge a group of the required 1s, ignoring the rest
Always treat all six as 1 to maximize coverage
Always treat all six as 0 to be safe
Remove those six cells from the map entirely
items.explanation: Don't-cares are opportunistic: assign each X to 1 only if it grows a group covering real minterms; leave the others effectively 0. Forcing all to 1 can create needless terms; forcing all to 0 forfeits free reduction.

items.prompt: Why does adding a consensus term to a minimal SOP NOT change the function it computes?
choices: The consensus term is logically redundant — it is already implied by the existing terms, so it only changes timing behavior, not the truth table
It changes the function but only for don't-care inputs
It converts the SOP into a POS
It removes one of the original product terms automatically
items.explanation: By the consensus theorem the added term is implied by the others, so steady-state output is unchanged. Its only effect is to keep coverage continuous during an input transition, killing the glitch.

items.prompt: How is a 5-variable function typically mapped with K-maps, and why is this near the practical limit?
choices: As two stacked 4-variable maps with adjacency between corresponding cells; tracking inter-map adjacency by eye becomes error-prone, so tabular/automated methods take over
As a single 5x6 grid with all 32 cells in one rectangle
It cannot be represented by any K-map at all
By converting it to decimal first
items.explanation: A 5-variable map is two 4-variable maps; cells in the same position across the maps are adjacent. Beyond 4-5 variables this 3D adjacency is hard to see reliably, which is why Quine-McCluskey / Espresso are used.

items.prompt: In Quine-McCluskey, which terms become the prime implicants?
choices: The terms that can no longer be combined with any other term (left unchecked at the end of all combining passes)
The terms appearing in the first column only
Every minterm that was originally listed
items.explanation: Combining proceeds until no further pairs differ in exactly one bit. Any term that was never absorbed into a larger combination is maximal — a prime implicant.

items.prompt: Why does hazard-free covering matter most for asynchronous outputs or signals driving edge-sensitive inputs?
choices: A transient glitch can be latched as a real edge/level by downstream logic, causing a false trigger that a synchronous register might otherwise have masked
Asynchronous logic runs slower so glitches last longer
Edge-sensitive inputs ignore all glitches by design
Glitches only affect power consumption, never function
items.explanation: Synchronous logic samples on a clock edge, often after glitches settle. But an asynchronous path or an edge-triggered input can capture the spurious transition as a genuine event, so the redundant consensus term is worth its gate.

lessons.title: From Combinational to Stateful: The SR Latch
lessons.content:
Combinational logic is memoryless: outputs are a pure function of present inputs, $Q = f(\text{inputs})$. The moment you feed an output back to an input, you create **memory** — the circuit's output now depends on its history. That feedback is the whole leap into *sequential* logic.

The simplest memory cell is the **SR latch**. In the NOR form, two cross-coupled NOR gates produce complementary outputs $Q$ and $\bar{Q}$. Asserting $S$ (Set) drives $Q\!=\!1$; asserting $R$ (Reset) drives $Q\!=\!0$. With $S\!=\!R\!=\!0$ the latch *holds* its last value — that is the storage. The NAND form is the dual: it uses active-low $\bar{S}$/$\bar{R}$ inputs.

The trap is the **forbidden state**. For the NOR latch, $S\!=\!R\!=\!1$ forces *both* outputs to 0, so $Q$ and $\bar{Q}$ are no longer complements — the invariant is broken. Worse, if both inputs then drop to 0 simultaneously, the latch enters a **race**: whichever gate is marginally faster wins, so the final state is unpredictable and can even oscillate. As an ex-radar tech, picture this as a comparator with positive feedback that hasn't decided which rail to slam to.

The behavior is summarized by the characteristic equation of the SR latch:

$$Q_{\text{next}} = S + \bar{R}\,Q \quad (\text{with } SR = 0 \text{ required})$$

The constraint $SR=0$ is exactly the promise to never enter the forbidden state. Everything richer — gated latches, flip-flops, registers — is built by disciplining *when* an SR-like core is allowed to change.

items.front: What single structural change turns combinational logic into sequential (stateful) logic?
items.back: Feedback: routing an output back to an input creates memory, so the output depends on past inputs, not just present ones.
items.hint: Memoryless vs. memory.

items.front: In a NOR-based SR latch, what do S=0, R=0 do?
items.back: Hold (latch retains its previous Q). This 'no change' mode is the actual storage behavior.
items.hint: Neither set nor reset is asserted.

items.front: Why is S=R=1 the 'forbidden' state of a NOR SR latch?
items.back: It forces both Q and Q-bar to 0, violating the complementary-output invariant; and releasing both inputs together causes an unpredictable race.
items.hint: What happens to the invariant Q = NOT(Q-bar)?

items.front: State the characteristic equation of the SR latch.
items.back: $Q_{\\text{next}} = S + \\bar{R}\\,Q$, valid under the constraint $SR = 0$.
items.hint: Set forces 1; otherwise hold unless reset.

items.front: How does the NAND SR latch differ from the NOR form at its inputs?
items.back: The NAND latch uses active-low inputs (S-bar, R-bar); asserting an input means driving it to 0, and S-bar=R-bar=0 is its forbidden state.
items.hint: Active-low duality.

items.prompt: What fundamentally distinguishes sequential logic from combinational logic?
choices: Sequential logic uses only NAND gates
Sequential logic outputs depend on input history via feedback/state
Combinational logic cannot use XOR gates
Sequential logic is always slower than combinational logic
items.explanation: Sequential logic stores state; its output depends on present inputs plus stored history, enabled by feedback. Gate choice and speed are unrelated to the distinction.

items.prompt: For a NOR-based SR latch, which input combination is the forbidden state?
items.explanation: S=R=1 drives both outputs to 0 (breaking complementarity) and leads to an unpredictable race if both inputs are released simultaneously.

items.prompt: With S=0 and R=0 applied to a stable NOR SR latch, the output Q will:
items.explanation: The hold condition retains the stored bit. Oscillation is a risk only when leaving the forbidden state, not during normal hold.

items.prompt: Why is leaving the SR forbidden state dangerous even after you stop asserting it?
choices: Both inputs releasing at once causes a race; the final state is determined by tiny gate-delay differences
It converts the latch into a combinational gate
items.explanation: From the all-driven state, simultaneous release makes the two cross-coupled gates race; whichever is marginally faster wins, so the settled state is unpredictable.

lessons.title: Level vs. Edge: Gated D Latches and D Flip-Flops
lessons.content:
An SR latch with a free-running data input is awkward, so we add a control. A **gated D latch** ties $S$ and $R$ to a single data line $D$ and its complement, both qualified by an **enable** (often called the gate or clock level). The characteristic behavior is **level-sensitive**:

$$Q_{\text{next}} = \begin{cases} D & \text{when EN} = 1 \\ Q & \text{when EN} = 0 \end{cases}$$

While $EN=1$ the latch is **transparent** — $Q$ follows $D$ continuously, glitches and all. When $EN$ falls, the latch *holds* whatever $D$ was at that instant. Transparency is exactly what makes raw latches hazardous in synchronous design: data can ripple through several open latches in one phase.

The fix is **edge triggering**. A **D flip-flop** captures $D$ only at one clock *transition* (conventionally the rising edge), then holds for the entire rest of the cycle. The classic implementation is the **master–slave** pair: two gated D latches with opposite-phase enables. While the clock is low, the *master* is transparent and tracks $D$ while the *slave* holds. On the rising edge the master freezes and the slave becomes transparent, passing the master's captured value to $Q$. The net effect is a single, clean sample per clock — see the timing diagram, where $Q$ updates only at rising edges.

This is *the* distinction to internalize: a **latch is level-sensitive (transparent while enabled)**, a **flip-flop is edge-sensitive (samples once per edge)**. The flip-flop is the fundamental storage element of synchronous design precisely because its update instant is unambiguous, which makes timing analysis tractable.

items.front: Define 'transparent' for a gated D latch.
items.back: While the enable is asserted (EN=1), Q follows D continuously in real time. When EN deasserts, Q holds the last D value.
items.hint: Level-sensitive while enabled.

items.front: What is the one-sentence difference between a latch and a flip-flop?
items.back: A latch is level-sensitive (transparent while enabled); a flip-flop is edge-triggered (samples its input only at a clock edge).
items.hint: Level vs. edge.

items.front: How does a master-slave D flip-flop achieve edge triggering?
items.back: Two gated D latches with opposite-phase enables: while CLK=0 the master tracks D and the slave holds; on the rising edge the master freezes and the slave passes the captured value out. One clean sample per cycle.
items.hint: Two latches, opposite enables.

items.front: Write the characteristic equation of a D flip-flop.
items.back: $Q_{\\text{next}} = D$, sampled at the active clock edge (Q holds between edges).
items.hint: It just stores D, but only at the edge.

items.front: Why are transparent latches risky in a clocked datapath?
items.back: During the transparent phase, data can ripple through multiple open latches in a single clock phase, defeating the one-update-per-clock assumption and complicating timing.
items.hint: Think 'race through' during the high phase.

items.prompt: A gated D latch has EN=1 the whole time D toggles. What does Q do?
choices: Q ignores D until the next clock edge
Q latches D only once and freezes
items.explanation: A latch is level-sensitive; while enabled it is transparent and Q tracks D in real time.

items.prompt: In a master-slave positive-edge D flip-flop, when is the value of D actually captured?
choices: At the rising edge of CLK
At the falling edge of CLK
items.explanation: On the rising edge the master stops tracking D (freezing the captured value) and the slave passes it to the output, so capture is effectively at the rising edge.

items.prompt: Why is the edge-triggered flip-flop preferred as the storage element of synchronous design?
choices: It uses fewer transistors than a latch
It can store two bits at once
Its update instant is unambiguous (one edge per cycle), making timing analysis tractable
It does not require a clock
items.explanation: A single, well-defined update instant per clock cycle is what makes setup/hold timing analysis and synchronous discipline work. (Latches actually use fewer transistors.)

items.prompt: Looking at a CLK/D/Q timing diagram, you see Q change value only at the instants CLK rises. This indicates the device is a:
items.explanation: Updates confined to rising edges are the signature of a positive-edge-triggered flip-flop; a latch would show Q tracking D throughout the enable level.

lessons.title: JK, T, Resets, Registers & Shift Registers
lessons.content:
The D flip-flop is the workhorse, but two relatives matter. The **JK flip-flop** generalizes SR with no forbidden state: $J\!=\!K\!=\!1$ **toggles**. Its characteristic equation is

$$Q_{\text{next}} = J\bar{Q} + \bar{K}Q.$$

Tie $J\!=\!K\!=\!1$ (or $J\!=\!K\!=\!T$) and you get the **T (toggle) flip-flop**: $Q_{\text{next}} = T \oplus Q$. T flip-flops are the natural building block of counters because each toggles at half the rate of the one feeding it.

**Set/reset timing matters.** An **asynchronous** preset/clear acts *immediately*, independent of the clock — useful for power-on init but dangerous mid-operation because it can violate timing and recovery/removal windows. A **synchronous** reset only takes effect at the active clock edge, keeping the device inside the synchronous discipline. The modern default is *asynchronous-assert, synchronous-deassert* for the global reset, and synchronous reset everywhere else.

**Registers** are just $N$ flip-flops sharing one clock — a **parallel-load** register captures an $N$-bit word in one edge. **Shift registers** wire each flip-flop's $Q$ to the next one's $D$ (see the 4-bit diagram), moving data one stage per clock. The access-pattern taxonomy: **SISO** (serial-in serial-out, a delay line), **SIPO** (serial-in parallel-out, deserializer), **PISO** (parallel-in serial-out, serializer), and PIPO (a plain register). Add XOR feedback from selected taps and a shift register becomes a **linear-feedback shift register (LFSR)**: an $n$-bit maximal LFSR cycles through $2^n - 1$ states, generating pseudorandom sequences used for scramblers, CRCs, and radar pseudo-noise codes.

items.front: What does a JK flip-flop do when J=K=1, and why is it 'better' than SR here?
items.back: It toggles (Q -> NOT Q). Unlike SR's S=R=1, this is well-defined, so JK has no forbidden state.
items.hint: SR's bad corner becomes useful.

items.front: Give the characteristic equations for the JK and T flip-flops.
items.back: JK: $Q_{\\text{next}} = J\\bar{Q} + \\bar{K}Q$. T: $Q_{\\text{next}} = T \\oplus Q$ (toggle when T=1).
items.hint: T is JK with J=K tied together.

items.front: Asynchronous vs. synchronous reset: what is the difference?
items.back: Async reset takes effect immediately, independent of the clock; synchronous reset only takes effect at the active clock edge. Modern best practice: async-assert, sync-deassert for the global reset.
items.hint: Does it wait for an edge?

items.front: Define SISO, SIPO, and PISO shift registers.
items.back: SISO: serial-in, serial-out (delay line). SIPO: serial-in, parallel-out (deserializer). PISO: parallel-in, serial-out (serializer).
items.hint: First letters = input form, last = output form.

items.front: What is an LFSR and how long is a maximal-length sequence for n bits?
items.back: A shift register with XOR feedback from selected taps; a maximal LFSR cycles through $2^n - 1$ states (all but all-zeros), producing a pseudorandom sequence used for scramblers, CRCs, and PN codes.
items.hint: All states except one.

items.prompt: A T flip-flop with T held at 1 produces an output that:
choices: Toggles every active clock edge (half the clock frequency)
items.explanation: T=1 toggles Q every edge, so Q is a divide-by-2 of the clock. This is why T flip-flops chain naturally into ripple counters.

items.prompt: Which structure converts a serial bit stream into a parallel word?
items.explanation: Serial-in parallel-out (SIPO) clocks bits in one at a time, then presents all N stage outputs in parallel — a deserializer.

items.prompt: An asynchronous (direct) clear on a flip-flop is risky during normal operation primarily because it:
choices: Acts independently of the clock and can violate recovery/removal timing, falling outside synchronous discipline
items.explanation: Async resets act immediately regardless of the clock, so their assertion/deassertion relative to the edge must obey recovery/removal windows; uncontrolled use breaks synchronous timing analysis.

items.prompt: A maximal-length 8-bit LFSR cycles through how many distinct states before repeating?
items.explanation: A maximal LFSR visits 2^n - 1 = 2^8 - 1 = 255 states; the all-zeros state is excluded because it is a lock-up (XOR feedback would keep it at zero).

lessons.title: Counters & the Synchronous Design Discipline
lessons.content:
**Counters** come in two flavors. An **asynchronous (ripple) counter** chains toggle flip-flops so each stage clocks the next; the count *ripples* through. It is cheap but the propagation delays **accumulate** — for $n$ stages the worst-case settling is $\approx n \cdot t_{cq}$, producing transient wrong codes (decoding glitches) and limiting $f_{max}$. A **synchronous counter** clocks *every* flip-flop from the same clock and uses combinational next-state logic so all bits update together; settling is one $t_{cq}+t_{pd}$ regardless of width. Always prefer synchronous.

**Modulo-N** counters count $0 \ldots N-1$ then wrap. A BCD counter is mod-10. You build mod-N by detecting the terminal count and synchronously loading/clearing. **Ring** and **Johnson** counters are shift-register counters: a ring circulates a single 1 through $n$ stages ($n$ states, one-hot, no decode logic); a **Johnson** (twisted-ring) feeds back the *inverted* last output, giving $2n$ states with simple 2-input decode.

The capstone discipline: **synchronous design**. One clock, every state element registered on the same edge, all logic between registers purely combinational. Two cardinal rules. First, **never gate the clock** — do not AND a clock with an enable. Gating injects skew, glitches, and creates a new (untimed) clock domain. Instead use a **clock enable**: a synchronous mux on the flip-flop's $D$ input that recirculates $Q$ when disabled:

$$D = EN \,?\, D_{\text{new}} : Q.$$

The clock runs free; only data is gated. Second, anything crossing into this clock domain from another (or from the async world) needs a synchronizer. Adopt this discipline and FPGA timing closure (Module 8) becomes tractable rather than a game of whack-a-mole.

items.front: Ripple vs. synchronous counter: what is the key timing difference?
items.back: In a ripple counter each stage clocks the next, so delays accumulate (~n*t_cq) and produce decode glitches. In a synchronous counter all flip-flops share one clock and update together, settling in one t_cq + t_pd regardless of width.
items.hint: Do the delays add up?

items.front: Why must you never gate a clock with an enable signal?
items.back: Gating injects skew and glitches and effectively creates a new, untimed clock domain, wrecking timing closure. Use a synchronous clock enable instead.
items.hint: It corrupts the clock tree.

items.front: What is the correct way to implement a clock enable on a D flip-flop?
items.back: Keep the clock free-running and place a synchronous mux on D that recirculates Q when disabled: $D = EN\\,?\\,D_{\\text{new}} : Q$. Only data is gated, never the clock.
items.hint: Mux on the data input, not the clock.

items.front: Ring counter vs. Johnson (twisted-ring) counter: states and feedback?
items.back: Ring: circulates a single 1; n stages give n one-hot states, no decode logic. Johnson: feeds back the inverted last output; n stages give 2n states with simple 2-input decode.
items.hint: Johnson inverts the feedback to double the states.

items.front: State the three pillars of synchronous design discipline.
items.back: One clock; every state element registered on the same edge; all inter-register logic purely combinational (plus: never gate clocks, synchronize anything crossing domains).
items.hint: One clock, everything registered.

items.front: How do you build a mod-N counter from a standard binary counter?
items.back: Detect the terminal count (N-1, or N) combinationally and synchronously load/clear back to 0 on the next edge, so it counts 0..N-1 and wraps.
items.hint: Detect-and-reset synchronously.

items.prompt: Why does a wide asynchronous ripple counter produce transient incorrect output codes?
choices: Each stage's clock comes from the previous stage, so bit transitions ripple and momentarily show invalid intermediate codes
The clock frequency is too low
items.explanation: Because stages clock one another, the bits do not change simultaneously; during the ripple the output passes through wrong intermediate values (decode glitches).

items.prompt: What is the recommended alternative to ANDing a clock with an enable to stop a register from updating?
choices: A synchronous clock enable: a mux on D that recirculates Q while the clock runs free
A tri-state buffer on the clock line
items.explanation: Gating the clock adds skew/glitches and a new clock domain. The clean fix keeps the clock free-running and selects between new data and the held value on the D input.

items.prompt: A 4-stage Johnson (twisted-ring) counter has how many distinct states?
items.explanation: A Johnson counter of n stages produces 2n states; for n=4 that is 8. A plain 4-stage ring counter would give only 4 (one-hot) states.

items.prompt: Which statement best captures synchronous design discipline?
choices: Use as many independent clocks as convenient and gate them per block
One clock edge updates all registers; combinational logic only sits between registers; cross-domain signals are synchronized
Prefer ripple counters and asynchronous resets everywhere for speed
Avoid registers and rely on transparent latches
items.explanation: Single clock, everything registered on one edge, purely combinational logic between registers, no gated clocks, and synchronizers at domain crossings — this is what makes timing closure tractable.

lessons.title: What an FSM Is: States, Diagrams, and Tables
lessons.content:
A **finite state machine (FSM)** is the formal model for any sequential system: at every clock edge it sits in one of a finite set of *states*, reads its *inputs*, and decides both its *next state* and its *outputs*. Mathematically it is the tuple $(S, I, O, \delta, \lambda, s_0)$ — states, inputs, outputs, the next-state function $\delta: S \times I \to S$, the output function $\lambda$, and a reset state $s_0$.

You already know this pattern from radar: a timing controller cycling through *idle → transmit → listen → process* is an FSM, and so is the PRI/PRF sequencer. In firmware it shows up as a `switch` on a `state` variable; in hardware it is flip-flops (the state register) plus combinational logic.

Two views capture the same machine. The **state diagram** is the graph: circles are states, directed arcs are transitions labeled by the input that triggers them. The **state/transition table** is the tabular form — one row per (current state, input) pair giving the next state and output. The table is what you hand to logic/HDL; the diagram is what you reason with.

A well-formed synchronous FSM has exactly one clock, every state change happens on the active clock edge, and the reset is explicit. The number of flip-flops needed is $\lceil \log_2 |S| \rceil$ for a binary encoding — so a 5-state machine needs 3 bits. Get the diagram right first; encoding and HDL come later. The discipline here — enumerate states, define every transition — is what stops the "it works until input X arrives" class of bug.

items.front: What six elements formally define a finite state machine?
items.back: States S, inputs I, outputs O, next-state function δ (S×I→S), output function λ, and an initial/reset state s₀.
items.hint: Think tuple: two sets you read, one you produce, two functions, one starting point.

items.front: What is the difference between a state diagram and a state/transition table?
items.back: They describe the same machine. The diagram is a graph (circles = states, arcs = transitions labeled by input) used for reasoning; the table lists, per (current state, input), the next state and output, and is what you translate into logic or HDL.
items.hint: One is visual, one is row-per-(state,input).

items.front: How many flip-flops does a binary-encoded FSM with N states need?
items.back: ⌈log₂ N⌉ flip-flops. For example, 5 states need 3 bits.
items.hint: Same as bits to count to N.

items.front: What are the rules of a well-formed synchronous FSM?
items.back: Exactly one clock; every state change occurs on the active clock edge; there is an explicit reset state. No gated clocks, no asynchronous state changes.
items.hint: One clock, edge-triggered, defined reset.

items.front: How does an FSM typically appear in C firmware?
items.back: As a switch statement on a state variable inside a loop: each case computes outputs and assigns the next value of the state variable. It is the same model as the hardware FSM.
items.hint: switch(state) { ... }

items.prompt: An FSM has 9 distinct states and uses a binary encoding. What is the minimum number of state flip-flops required?
items.explanation: ⌈log₂ 9⌉ = 4 bits (3 bits only count to 8). The remaining 16−9 = 7 codes are unused/illegal states.

items.prompt: In the FSM tuple, which function has the signature S × I → S?
items.explanation: δ (delta) maps the current state and current input to the next state. λ (lambda) is the output function.

items.prompt: A radar timing controller cycles idle → transmit → listen → process → idle. This is best modeled as:
items.explanation: Sequenced, state-dependent behavior driven by a clock and inputs is exactly an FSM — control sequencers like this are a canonical FSM application.

lessons.title: Moore vs Mealy and the Sequence Detector
lessons.content:
FSMs split into two output styles. In a **Moore** machine the output depends on the *state only*: $\lambda(s)$. In a **Mealy** machine the output depends on *state and current input*: $\lambda(s, i)$.

The trade-offs matter in practice. **Moore** outputs are written from the state register, so they are *registered, glitch-free, and synchronous* — ideal when an output drives something timing-sensitive. The cost is latency: an input change is reflected one clock later, and Moore machines often need *more states*. **Mealy** outputs respond *combinationally in the same cycle*, so they react faster and usually need *fewer states*, but they can glitch and they expose the output to input hazards. A common compromise is a Mealy machine with a registered output — you get Mealy's state economy plus a clean registered output.

The classic exercise is a **"1011" sequence detector with overlap**: assert $y=1$ for one cycle each time the serial bit stream ends in 1011, reusing the trailing 11 so the streams ...1011011... fire twice.

As a **Moore** machine you need states tracking progress: S0 (nothing), S1 (saw 1), S10 (saw 10), S101 (saw 101), S1011 (match, $y=1$) — five states, output asserted in S1011 only. As a **Mealy** machine you fold the match into the transition: from the S101 state, input 1 both moves you and asserts $y=1$ on that same edge — four states. The Moore diagram below shows the state-only output and the overlap arcs (a match returns toward S10/S101 rather than S0).

$$ \text{states}_{\text{Moore}} = 5, \qquad \text{states}_{\text{Mealy}} = 4 $$

items.front: In a Moore machine, what does the output depend on?
items.back: The current state only: output = λ(s). It does not depend directly on the current input.
items.hint: Output is a label on the state circle.

items.front: In a Mealy machine, what does the output depend on?
items.back: Both the current state and the current input: output = λ(s, i). Outputs are labeled on the transition arcs.
items.hint: Output is a label on the arrow, not the circle.

items.front: What are the advantages and costs of a Moore machine?
items.back: Advantages: outputs are registered, glitch-free, and synchronous. Costs: one-cycle output latency and usually more states than the equivalent Mealy machine.
items.hint: Clean but slower and bigger.

items.front: What are the advantages and costs of a Mealy machine?
items.back: Advantages: faster response (output changes the same cycle as the input) and usually fewer states. Costs: outputs can glitch and are exposed to input hazards because they are combinational.
items.hint: Fast and small but can glitch.

items.front: What is the common compromise between Moore and Mealy output styles?
items.back: A Mealy machine with a registered output: you keep Mealy's reduced state count but pass the output through a flip-flop to get a clean, glitch-free registered signal (at the cost of one cycle of delay).
items.hint: Mealy logic, but flop the output.

items.front: In a '1011' overlapping sequence detector, why doesn't a match return to the start state?
items.back: Because the trailing '11' of one match can begin the next pattern. After detecting 1011 the machine returns to the state representing having just seen the '1' (toward S101/S10), so streams like 1011011 fire on each completed 1011.
items.hint: The tail bits are reused as a head.

items.prompt: For an overlapping '1011' detector, the Moore version needs 5 states and the Mealy version needs 4. Why does Mealy need fewer?
choices: Mealy machines cannot detect overlapping patterns
The Mealy output is asserted on the final transition itself, so it does not need a dedicated 'matched' state
Mealy machines always use one-hot encoding
Moore machines cannot use a reset state
items.explanation: Moore needs a separate S1011 state to carry the output; Mealy folds the assertion into the arc into the would-be match, eliminating that state.

items.prompt: You must generate a clean, glitch-free strobe that gates another synchronous block. Which output style is the safest choice?
choices: Either one, glitches are irrelevant here
items.explanation: Moore outputs come straight from the state register and are glitch-free and synchronous — ideal for a strobe that must be clean. A combinational Mealy output can momentarily glitch.

items.prompt: Compared to a Mealy machine, a Moore machine's response to an input change is:
choices: Delayed, typically appearing one clock cycle later
items.explanation: Because a Moore output depends only on the state, the input must first change the state at a clock edge before the output reflects it — a one-cycle latency Mealy avoids.

lessons.title: State Encoding and Minimization
lessons.content:
Once the diagram is right, you assign each symbolic state a bit pattern — the **state encoding**. The choice changes the next-state/output logic, the flip-flop count, speed, and power.

- **Binary (sequential):** $\lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ flip-flops, fewest registers. Good when registers are scarce (e.g., discrete logic, small CPLDs). Next-state logic can be wide.
- **Gray:** like binary but adjacent states differ by one bit. Minimizes simultaneous bit flips, which cuts switching noise and is helpful when the *state bits themselves cross a clock domain* (the same reason Gray code is used in CDC and encoders).
- **One-hot:** one flip-flop per state, exactly one bit high at a time (e.g., 0001, 0010, 0100, 1000). Uses the most flip-flops but the *next-state logic is trivially simple and fast* — each state's enable is a small AND of a single bit and an input.

**Why one-hot wins on FPGAs:** FPGAs are flip-flop-rich (every logic cell has a register) but the combinational LUTs are the scarce, delay-dominating resource. One-hot trades cheap flip-flops for shallow, fast next-state logic, raising $f_{max}$. Synthesizers default to one-hot for FPGA targets and binary for ASIC/CPLD targets.

**State minimization** removes redundant states before encoding. Two states are *equivalent* if, for every input sequence, they produce identical outputs and transition to equivalent states. Merging equivalent states (via an implication chart or partition refinement) shrinks the register count and logic. Modern synthesis does this automatically, but recognizing equivalent states keeps your hand-drawn diagrams honest and your HDL `case` statements lean.

$$ \text{one-hot FFs} = N, \qquad \text{binary FFs} = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil $$

items.front: What is binary (sequential) state encoding and its main advantage?
items.back: States are numbered 0,1,2,... in straight binary, using ⌈log₂ N⌉ flip-flops. Advantage: the fewest flip-flops, good when registers are scarce. Downside: next-state logic can be wide/slow.
items.hint: Fewest registers.

items.front: Why might you use Gray-code state encoding?
items.back: Adjacent states differ by exactly one bit, minimizing simultaneous bit transitions. This reduces switching noise and is valuable if the state bits themselves must cross a clock domain (same rationale as Gray code in CDC/encoders).
items.hint: One bit changes at a time.

items.front: What is one-hot encoding?
items.back: One flip-flop per state with exactly one bit asserted at a time (e.g., 0001, 0010, 0100, 1000). Uses N flip-flops but yields very simple, fast next-state logic.
items.hint: Exactly one '1' bit per state.

items.front: Why is one-hot encoding preferred on FPGAs?
items.back: FPGAs are flip-flop-rich (a register per logic cell) but LUT logic depth dominates delay. One-hot spends cheap flip-flops to make the next-state logic shallow and fast, raising f_max. Synthesizers default to one-hot for FPGA targets.
items.hint: FFs are cheap, LUT depth is expensive.

items.front: When are two FSM states equivalent (and mergeable)?
items.back: When, for every possible input sequence, they produce identical outputs and transition to equivalent next states. Equivalent states can be merged to reduce the state count.
items.hint: Same outputs and same onward behavior, forever.

items.prompt: A 6-state FSM is implemented one-hot. How many state flip-flops does it use?
items.explanation: One-hot uses one flip-flop per state, so 6 states need 6 flip-flops. Binary would need only ⌈log₂ 6⌉ = 3.

items.prompt: Which statement best captures the binary-vs-one-hot trade-off?
choices: Binary uses fewer flip-flops; one-hot uses simpler/faster next-state logic
One-hot always uses fewer flip-flops and less logic
Binary is always faster on every technology
They are identical in resource use
items.explanation: Binary minimizes register count; one-hot minimizes logic depth at the cost of more registers — which is why one-hot suits FF-rich FPGAs.

items.prompt: If FSM state bits must be read in another clock domain, which encoding most reduces metastability risk?
choices: Binary, because it uses fewer bits
One-hot, because only one bit is ever high
Gray, because only one bit changes per transition
Encoding has no effect on CDC
items.explanation: Gray encoding guarantees a single bit changes per transition, so a sample taken during the change yields either the old or new value — never a corrupt multi-bit intermediate. This is the standard CDC trick for multi-bit values.

items.prompt: What is the primary benefit of FSM state minimization?
choices: It increases the clock frequency by definition
It removes redundant equivalent states, reducing flip-flops and logic
It converts a Mealy machine into a Moore machine
items.explanation: Minimization merges behaviorally equivalent states, shrinking the state set and therefore the register and next-state-logic cost.

lessons.title: Three-Block HDL, Illegal-State Recovery, and FSMD
lessons.content:
The professional way to code an FSM is the **three-block (three-process) structure**, which separates concerns the synthesizer and reader both expect:

1. **State register** — a clocked block, non-blocking assignments, with reset: `always @(posedge clk) if (rst) state <= S0; else state <= next;`
2. **Next-state logic** — a purely combinational block (`always @(*)`, blocking `=`) that computes `next` from `state` and inputs. Always include a `default` so no state is left unassigned (an incomplete `case` infers an unwanted latch).
3. **Output logic** — combinational for Mealy, or driven from `state` for Moore. Keeping it separate makes the Moore/Mealy choice explicit.

This maps directly onto the diagram: block 2 is $\delta$, block 3 is $\lambda$, block 1 is the memory. The same skeleton becomes a C `switch` in firmware.

**Illegal-state recovery:** with one-hot encoding, $N$ states use only $N$ of $2^N$ codes; even binary leaves $2^{\lceil\log_2 N\rceil} - N$ unused codes. A glitch, single-event upset, or power transient can land the register in an undefined code. The `default` branch of the next-state `case` must steer any unlisted code back to a safe state (usually reset/idle). For high-reliability work, declare an explicit recovery and avoid `full_case`/`parallel_case` pragmas that *tell the tool to ignore* those codes.

**FSMD (FSM with datapath):** real designs pair a control FSM with a datapath (registers, ALUs, counters, memories). The FSM issues control signals — load, shift, add, mux-select — and reads status flags (zero, done, overflow) back from the datapath to decide transitions. This control/datapath partition is the bridge from toy detectors to actual processors and the capstone acquisition pipeline: an idle→acquire→process→output controller sequencing a FIR datapath is exactly an FSMD.

items.front: What are the three blocks of the canonical three-block FSM HDL structure?
items.back: (1) State register — clocked, with reset; (2) next-state logic — combinational, computes next from state+inputs; (3) output logic — from state (Moore) or state+inputs (Mealy).
items.hint: Memory, δ, λ.

items.front: Which assignment type goes in the clocked state-register block, and which in the combinational next-state block?
items.back: Clocked block (always @(posedge clk)): non-blocking (<=). Combinational block (always @(*)): blocking (=). Mixing them up is the #1 HDL trap.
items.hint: Non-blocking for clocked, blocking for combinational.

items.front: Why must the combinational next-state case include a default (or assign next at the top)?
items.back: An incomplete case leaves 'next' unassigned for some inputs, so the synthesizer infers a latch to hold the old value — an unintended, hazard-prone storage element. A default assignment avoids it.
items.hint: Incomplete case → inferred latch.

items.front: How do you make an FSM recover from an illegal/undefined state?
items.back: Route every unlisted state code (the default branch of the next-state case) back to a safe state such as reset/idle. Avoid full_case/parallel_case pragmas that tell the tool to treat those codes as don't-cares.
items.hint: default → go to a safe state.

items.front: What is an FSMD (FSM with datapath)?
items.back: A control FSM coupled to a datapath (registers, ALUs, counters, memories). The FSM issues control signals (load, shift, mux-select) and reads status flags (zero, done, overflow) to decide transitions — the control/datapath split underlying real processors.
items.hint: Controller + datapath, talking via control and status signals.

items.front: Why are illegal states even possible in a one-hot or binary FSM?
items.back: The encoding spans more codes than there are valid states (one-hot: N of 2^N; binary: 2^⌈log₂N⌉ − N spare codes). Glitches, single-event upsets, or power transients can drop the register into one of these undefined codes.
items.hint: Unused codes exist; upsets can reach them.

items.prompt: In the three-block style, the state register update inside always @(posedge clk) should use:
items.explanation: Clocked sequential logic uses non-blocking (<=) so all registers update from their pre-edge values simultaneously, matching real flip-flop behavior. Blocking in clocked logic causes simulate/synthesize mismatches.

items.prompt: A combinational next-state always @(*) block has a case with no default and leaves 'next' unassigned for some states. What does synthesis most likely infer?
choices: An unintended latch holding the old value
items.explanation: When a combinational output is not assigned on every path, the tool must remember the previous value, so it infers a transparent latch — a classic, hazard-prone bug. A default fixes it.

items.prompt: For an illegal-state-recoverable FSM, the safest treatment of unused state codes is to:
choices: Mark them don't-care with full_case/parallel_case pragmas
Leave them unassigned to save logic
Have the default branch transition to a known safe state (e.g., reset/idle)
Tie the state register to an asynchronous latch
items.explanation: Explicitly steering any undefined code to a safe state guarantees recovery. The full_case/parallel_case pragmas do the opposite — they let the tool optimize those codes away, removing the recovery path.

items.prompt: In an FSMD, how does the control FSM decide some of its transitions based on computation results?
choices: It samples status flags (e.g., zero, done, overflow) fed back from the datapath
It cannot react to the datapath at all
items.explanation: The datapath returns status signals (zero, done, carry/overflow, comparison results) to the FSM, which uses them as transition conditions — e.g., loop until a counter's done flag asserts.

lessons.title: Delays & Flip-Flop Timing Parameters
lessons.content:
Every gate and wire takes time to respond. **Propagation delay** $t_{pd}$ is the *maximum* time from a stable input change to a stable output; **contamination delay** $t_{cd}$ is the *minimum* time before the output *starts* to change. So after an input edge the output is guaranteed stable only after $t_{pd}$, and guaranteed unchanged only up to $t_{cd}$. Note $t_{cd} \le t_{pd}$ always.

A flip-flop adds three timing parameters that define a window around its clock edge:

- **Setup time** $t_{su}$ — the input $D$ must be stable for at least $t_{su}$ *before* the active clock edge.
- **Hold time** $t_{h}$ — $D$ must remain stable for at least $t_{h}$ *after* the edge.
- **Clock-to-Q delay** $t_{cq}$ — after the edge, $Q$ is valid after $t_{cq}$ (datasheets often give both $t_{cq,max}$ and a $t_{cq,min}$, akin to $t_{pd}$ vs $t_{cd}$ for combinational logic).

Violate $t_{su}$ or $t_{h}$ and the flop may capture a wrong value or go *metastable* (Lesson 4). As a radar tech you measured rise/fall times on a scope; these are the same physics, now abstracted into datasheet numbers your timing tools consume.

Think of a register-to-register path: the launching flop fires ($t_{cq}$), the signal crosses combinational logic ($t_{pd}$), and must arrive at the capturing flop's $D$ before its $t_{su}$. That chain is the seed of every timing constraint in the rest of this subtopic.

items.front: Define propagation delay $t_{pd}$.
items.back: The **maximum** time from an input change until the output is **guaranteed stable** at its new value. It bounds the worst-case (slowest) path.
items.hint: Worst case, output settled.

items.front: Define contamination delay $t_{cd}$ and its relation to $t_{pd}$.
items.back: The **minimum** time before an output **begins** to change after an input change. Always $t_{cd} \le t_{pd}$. It bounds the fastest path and matters for hold timing.
items.hint: Earliest the output can move.

items.front: What is setup time $t_{su}$?
items.back: The minimum time the data input $D$ must be **stable before** the active clock edge for the flip-flop to capture it reliably.
items.hint: Stable before the edge.

items.front: What is hold time $t_{h}$?
items.back: The minimum time $D$ must remain **stable after** the active clock edge for reliable capture.
items.hint: Stable after the edge.

items.front: What is clock-to-Q delay $t_{cq}$?
items.back: The delay from the active clock edge until the flip-flop output $Q$ is valid. It is the time the launching flop adds to a register-to-register path.
items.hint: Edge to valid output.

items.prompt: Data must be held stable for a window around the clock edge. Which two parameters bound that window?
items.explanation: Setup time $t_{su}$ is measured before the edge and hold time $t_{h}$ after it; together they form the keep-out window for $D$.

items.prompt: Which inequality between a gate's delays is always true?
items.explanation: The output cannot finish settling ($t_{pd}$) before it even starts to change ($t_{cd}$), so $t_{cd} \le t_{pd}$.

items.prompt: On a register-to-register path, which delay does the launching flip-flop contribute before the signal enters the combinational logic?
items.explanation: After the launch edge, $Q$ becomes valid only after $t_{cq}$; that is the launching flop's contribution to the path delay.

items.prompt: A datasheet lists $t_{cq,max}$ and $t_{cq,min}$. Why distinguish them?
choices: They are the same number written twice
$t_{cq,max}$ feeds worst-case (setup) analysis; $t_{cq,min}$ feeds fastest-path (hold) analysis
$t_{cq,min}$ is used only for power estimation
$t_{cq,max}$ applies only to the clock tree
items.explanation: Setup checks use the slowest (max) launch, while hold checks use the fastest (min) launch—analogous to using $t_{pd}$ vs $t_{cd}$ in combinational logic.

lessons.title: The Setup & Hold Inequalities, f_max, and the Critical Path
lessons.content:
Consider two flip-flops separated by combinational logic, both on clock period $T_{clk}$. After a launch edge the data is valid at $D$ of the capturing flop at time $t_{cq}+t_{pd}$, and it must arrive at least $t_{su}$ before the *next* edge. That gives the **setup constraint**:

$$t_{cq} + t_{pd,max} + t_{su} \le T_{clk}$$

Solving for the period sets the **maximum clock frequency**:

$$f_{max} = \frac{1}{t_{cq} + t_{pd,max} + t_{su}}$$

The slowest register-to-register path—the **critical path**—is the one that maximizes $t_{cq}+t_{pd,max}$ and therefore caps $f_{max}$. **Slack** is how much margin a path has: $\text{slack} = T_{clk} - (t_{cq}+t_{pd,max}+t_{su})$; negative slack means a setup failure.

The **hold constraint** is different—it compares fastest data against the *same* edge:

$$t_{cq,min} + t_{cd} \ge t_{h}$$

Notice $T_{clk}$ does **not** appear. Hold is **frequency-independent**: the data launched at an edge must not race through and corrupt the capturing flop's input *before* its hold window closes after that same edge. This is why **slowing the clock can fix setup violations but never hold violations**—a classic rust-knocker. Hold failures are fixed by adding delay (buffers) on short paths or fixing skew, not by lowering frequency.

items.front: State the setup (max-delay) constraint for a register-to-register path.
items.back: $t_{cq} + t_{pd,max} + t_{su} \le T_{clk}$. The launched data plus worst-case logic plus setup must fit inside one clock period.
items.hint: Everything must fit in $T_{clk}$.

items.front: Give the formula for $f_{max}$ from setup timing (zero skew).
items.back: $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{t_{cq} + t_{pd,max} + t_{su}}$, set by the critical (slowest) path.
items.hint: Reciprocal of the longest path period.

items.front: State the hold (min-delay) constraint and note what is absent.
items.back: $t_{cq,min} + t_{cd} \ge t_{h}$. **No $T_{clk}$ term**—hold is frequency-independent.
items.hint: Fastest data vs. the same edge.

items.front: Define the critical path.
items.back: The register-to-register path with the largest $t_{cq}+t_{pd,max}$; it has the least setup slack and therefore determines $f_{max}$.
items.hint: Slowest path = lowest slack.

items.front: Why can't you fix a hold violation by slowing the clock?
items.back: The hold inequality $t_{cq,min}+t_{cd} \ge t_{h}$ has no $T_{clk}$ term. It's a race against the *same* edge, so a longer period does nothing—you must add path delay or fix skew.
items.hint: No period in the equation.

items.prompt: Given $t_{cq}=0.3\,\text{ns}$, $t_{pd,max}=4.2\,\text{ns}$, $t_{su}=0.5\,\text{ns}$ and zero skew, what is $f_{max}$?
A path has $T_{clk}=8\,\text{ns}$, $t_{cq}=0.4$, $t_{pd,max}=6.0$, $t_{su}=0.6\,\text{ns}$. What is the setup slack?
Which variable does NOT appear in the hold-time inequality?
items.explanation: Hold is $t_{cq,min}+t_{cd} \ge t_{h}$; the clock period is absent, which is exactly why hold is frequency-independent.

items.prompt: A design fails hold on a short path between two adjacent flops. What is a valid fix?
choices: Add buffer/delay on the data path or fix the skew
items.explanation: Hold violations need more minimum data delay (buffers) or skew correction; period changes have no effect because $T_{clk}$ is not in the inequality.

lessons.title: Clock Skew, Jitter, and Pipelining
lessons.content:
**Clock skew** is the difference in clock arrival time between the launching and capturing flops, $t_{skew} = t_{clk,capture} - t_{clk,launch}$. Skew shifts both constraints. With skew the setup constraint becomes

$$t_{cq} + t_{pd,max} + t_{su} \le T_{clk} + t_{skew}$$

Positive skew (clock reaches the capture flop *later*) *relaxes* setup—it effectively lends time. But the same positive skew *tightens* hold:

$$t_{cq,min} + t_{cd} \ge t_{h} + t_{skew}$$

So skew is a double-edged sword; tools budget it carefully, and uncontrolled skew (e.g., from gating a clock) is dangerous.

**Jitter** is cycle-to-cycle variation in the clock edge position—random (phase noise) and deterministic. Unlike static skew, jitter is uncertain, so it eats directly into your timing budget: subtract it from the period for setup. Your radar phase-noise intuition transfers exactly—jitter is phase noise in the time domain.

**Pipelining** raises throughput by inserting registers to chop one long combinational path into shorter stages. If a path of delay $D$ is split into $k$ balanced stages, each stage is $\approx D/k$, so $f_{max}$ rises (ideally by $\approx k\times$, minus the added $t_{cq}+t_{su}$ per stage). The cost is **latency**: results now take $k$ cycles to emerge, and you must add matching pipeline delays to side signals. Pipelining is the standard answer when a critical path won't meet timing—central to FPGA radar DSP, where deep MAC pipelines run at line rate.

items.front: Define clock skew.
items.back: $t_{skew} = t_{clk,capture} - t_{clk,launch}$: the difference in clock arrival time between the capturing and launching flip-flops.
items.hint: Capture-edge arrival minus launch-edge arrival.

items.front: How does positive skew affect the setup and hold constraints?
items.back: Positive skew **relaxes setup** ($\dots \le T_{clk}+t_{skew}$) but **tightens hold** ($t_{cq,min}+t_{cd} \ge t_{h}+t_{skew}$). It's a trade-off, not free time.
items.hint: Helps one, hurts the other.

items.front: How does clock jitter differ from skew, and how is it budgeted?
items.back: Skew is a fixed spatial offset; jitter is random/cycle-to-cycle uncertainty in edge timing. Jitter is subtracted from the usable period for setup analysis. It is phase noise viewed in the time domain.
items.hint: Static vs. uncertain.

items.front: What does pipelining do to $f_{max}$ and to latency?
items.back: Splitting a path into $k$ balanced stages raises $f_{max}$ by up to $\approx k\times$ (minus per-stage $t_{cq}+t_{su}$), but increases latency to $k$ cycles before the first result emerges.
items.hint: Higher throughput, more cycles of delay.

items.front: When pipelining a datapath, what must you do to parallel/side signals?
items.back: Delay them by matching pipeline registers so they stay aligned with the data they accompany; otherwise control/data become misaligned by the pipeline depth.
items.hint: Keep everything in step, stage for stage.

items.prompt: With positive clock skew $t_{skew}$, the setup constraint becomes:
items.explanation: Positive skew delays the capture edge, effectively adding $t_{skew}$ to the available period and relaxing setup.

items.prompt: A 12 ns combinational path is pipelined into 3 balanced stages of 4 ns each (ignore added $t_{cq}/t_{su}$). The new throughput period is roughly:
items.explanation: Each stage is now the critical path at ~4 ns, so the clock period drops to ~4 ns even though end-to-end latency is still ~12 ns (3 cycles).

items.prompt: Why is gating a clock with an AND-enable considered bad practice for timing?
choices: It introduces uncontrolled skew/glitches on the clock and complicates timing closure
It is the only way to reduce hold violations
items.explanation: Logic in the clock path adds skew and can glitch the clock; use a synchronous clock-enable on the flop instead.

items.prompt: Clock jitter most directly hurts which budget?
choices: It eats into the effective period, tightening the setup budget
It has no effect on synchronous timing
items.explanation: Because the next edge may arrive early, jitter is subtracted from the usable period, shrinking setup margin (just as phase noise degrades a radar's coherence).

lessons.title: Metastability, Synchronizers, CDC & Reset
lessons.content:
When a flip-flop's setup/hold window is violated—inevitable when sampling a signal asynchronous to the clock—its output can hover between logic levels for an unbounded time before resolving randomly. This is **metastability**. It is a physical bistable-circuit phenomenon (a ball balanced on a hill), so it can never be fully eliminated, only made statistically rare.

The figure of merit is **mean time between failures**:

$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{e^{\,t_{r}/\tau}}{f_{clk}\, f_{data}\, T_{0}}$$

where $t_{r}$ is the resolution time allowed before the value is used, $\tau$ and $T_0$ are device constants, and $f_{clk}$, $f_{data}$ are the clock and data-event rates. MTBF grows *exponentially* with the resolution time $t_{r}$—the lever we control.

The **two-flop synchronizer** buys one full clock of resolution time: FF1 samples the async input (and may go metastable), and FF2 resamples FF1's output one cycle later, by which time it has almost certainly resolved. That single-bit recipe is the foundation of **clock-domain crossing (CDC)**.

Multi-bit buses can't use parallel two-flop synchronizers—bits would resolve on different cycles, producing illegal intermediate values. Instead: encode counters in **Gray code** (only one bit changes per step, so at most one bit is mid-transition), use a **handshake** (req/ack with the data held stable), or use an **asynchronous FIFO** (the standard for streaming data, combining Gray-coded pointers and dual-clock RAM).

Finally, **reset strategy**: prefer **asynchronous assert, synchronous de-assert**. Assert reset immediately regardless of clock, but release it synchronized to the destination clock so flops don't exit reset near a clock edge and go metastable—the same synchronizer reasoning applied to the reset line itself.

items.front: What is metastability and why is it unavoidable?
items.back: A flip-flop output stuck between valid logic levels for an unbounded time after a setup/hold violation. It's an intrinsic bistable-circuit phenomenon (unstable equilibrium), so it can only be made rare, never eliminated.
items.hint: Ball balanced on a hill.

items.front: Write the metastability MTBF expression and name the lever you control.
items.back: $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{e^{\,t_{r}/\tau}}{f_{clk}\,f_{data}\,T_{0}}$. The controllable lever is the resolution time $t_{r}$; MTBF grows **exponentially** with it.
items.hint: Exponential in resolution time.

items.front: How does a two-flop synchronizer reduce failure rate?
items.back: FF1 samples the async signal and may go metastable; FF2 resamples FF1's output a full clock later, giving ~one period of resolution time ($t_r$), which raises MTBF exponentially.
items.hint: Second flop buys settling time.

items.front: Why can't you synchronize a multi-bit bus with parallel two-flop synchronizers?
items.back: Individual bits may resolve on different cycles, yielding illegal transient combinations. Use Gray-coded counters, a handshake, or an async FIFO instead.
items.hint: Bits resolve out of step.

items.front: State the recommended reset strategy and its rationale.
items.back: **Asynchronous assert, synchronous de-assert**: assert reset immediately, but release it synchronized to the destination clock so flops don't exit reset near an edge and go metastable.
items.hint: Assert now, release in sync.

items.front: Why is Gray code used for CDC counter (e.g., FIFO pointer) crossings?
items.back: Only one bit changes between consecutive Gray-code values, so at most one bit can be mid-transition when sampled—any captured value is either the old or new count, never an illegal in-between.
items.hint: One-bit-change-per-step property.

items.prompt: In the MTBF expression, increasing which quantity raises MTBF exponentially?
items.explanation: MTBF $\propto e^{t_r/\tau}$; only $t_r$ sits in the exponent. The rate terms in the denominator make MTBF worse as they grow.

items.prompt: What is the role of the FIRST flop in a two-flop synchronizer?
choices: It guarantees a glitch-free output immediately
It samples the async input and absorbs the (possible) metastable event, giving FF2 a full cycle to see a resolved value
items.explanation: FF1 is the one allowed to go metastable; the design relies on it resolving within one clock so FF2 captures a clean level.

items.prompt: You must stream a wide data word from a fast ADC clock domain to a slower processing domain. Best mechanism?
choices: Parallel two-flop synchronizers on every data bit
A single-bit synchronizer on bit 0 only
An asynchronous (dual-clock) FIFO with Gray-coded pointers
Gate the source clock with an enable
items.explanation: Streaming multi-bit data across domains is exactly what an async FIFO solves: data RAM is dual-clock and the read/write pointers cross safely via Gray code.

items.prompt: Why de-assert reset synchronously even when asserting it asynchronously?
choices: So flops don't release from reset too close to a clock edge and risk metastability
Because asynchronous assert is forbidden by all tools
To save the global reset routing resources
items.explanation: Async assert guarantees immediate reset regardless of clock state, while synchronized de-assert ensures all flops leave reset on a clean edge, avoiding a metastable exit.

lessons.title: Volatile Memory: SRAM vs DRAM and DDR
lessons.content:
The memory hierarchy trades speed for density and cost, and the first split is **volatile vs non-volatile**: volatile memory loses its contents when power drops. Two volatile families dominate.

**SRAM** stores one bit in a **6-transistor (6T) cell**: two cross-coupled inverters form a bistable latch, plus two access transistors gated by the **word line** that connect the cell to a differential **bit-line** pair (BL, BL'). Because the latch actively holds its state, SRAM needs **no refresh**, has a **non-destructive read**, and is very fast. The price is area: six transistors per bit means **low density** and high cost. That is exactly why SRAM is used where speed matters and capacity is modest, CPU caches and **FPGA block RAM**.

**DRAM** stores a bit as charge on a single capacitor, the **1T1C cell**: one access transistor plus one tiny capacitor. This is enormously dense and cheap, but the capacitor **leaks**, so every row must be periodically rewritten, the **refresh** requirement (typically every ~64 ms). Reading drains the capacitor, so the read is **destructive** and the sense amplifiers must **write the value back** after every access. DRAM is addressed in two phases, **row** then **column** (RAS/CAS), to share pins.

Modern **SDRAM/DDR** is synchronous to a clock and organized into **banks** so accesses can be interleaved. **Bursts** stream several words per command, and **CAS latency (CL)** is the clock-cycle delay from a read command to first data. DDR transfers on **both clock edges** (double data rate); successive generations (DDR2/3/4/5) raise bus rates and burst length while lowering voltage.

items.front: SRAM 6T cell vs DRAM 1T1C cell: structure and the key consequences of each.
items.back: Left, SRAM: two cross-coupled inverters (a bistable latch) plus two access transistors to a differential bit-line pair, gated by the word line. It actively holds state, so no refresh and non-destructive read, but six transistors per bit means low density. Right, DRAM: one transistor and one storage capacitor. Very dense and cheap, but the charge leaks (needs refresh) and reading discharges the cap (destructive read, requires write-back).

items.front: Why does DRAM need refresh while SRAM does not?
items.back: DRAM stores each bit as charge on a tiny capacitor (1T1C). Junction and subthreshold leakage drain that charge over milliseconds, so each row must be periodically read and rewritten (refresh, typically every ~64 ms) to keep the data valid. SRAM stores its bit in a cross-coupled inverter latch (6T) that is actively driven by the supply, so as long as power is present the state is regenerated continuously and no refresh is needed.
items.hint: Charge on a leaky cap vs an actively-driven latch.

items.front: What does 'destructive read' mean for DRAM, and how is it handled?
items.back: Sensing a DRAM cell connects its storage capacitor to the bit line, which shares/drains the cell's charge so the original value is lost (corrupted) by the act of reading. The sense amplifiers detect the small voltage swing, regenerate the full logic level, and write the value back into the row (precharge/restore) after every read. This is part of why DRAM has a row-cycle time and why a whole row is opened at once.
items.hint: Reading drains the cap; sense amps must write it back.

items.front: Why is DRAM addressed in two phases (RAS then CAS)?
items.back: To save package pins and cost, the address is multiplexed: the row address is presented first and latched by RAS (Row Address Strobe), opening an entire row into the sense amplifiers; then the column address is presented and latched by CAS (Column Address Strobe) to pick the word within that open row. This halves the number of address pins and matches DRAM's internal two-dimensional array organization.
items.hint: Pin saving; row strobe then column strobe.

items.front: In DDR SDRAM, what do 'double data rate', 'banks', 'burst', and 'CAS latency' each mean?
items.back: Double data rate: data is transferred on BOTH the rising and falling edges of the clock, doubling throughput for a given clock. Banks: independent sub-arrays that can have rows open simultaneously, so accesses interleave and hide row-open latency. Burst: one command streams several consecutive words (burst length) instead of one. CAS latency (CL): the number of clock cycles from issuing a read (column) command until the first data word appears.
items.hint: Both edges; interleaved arrays; multi-word streams; read-to-data delay.

items.prompt: A design needs a small, very fast on-chip scratchpad with single-cycle deterministic access and no controller overhead. Which memory fits best?
items.explanation: SRAM gives fast, deterministic, non-destructive single-cycle access with no refresh and no multi-phase addressing, which is exactly what an on-chip scratchpad or cache wants. DRAM adds refresh, RAS/CAS, and controller latency; NAND and EEPROM are non-volatile but far slower and block/page oriented.

items.prompt: Why is DRAM able to achieve much higher bit density than SRAM?
choices: Its 1-transistor-1-capacitor cell is far smaller than SRAM's 6-transistor cell
DRAM cells store multiple bits each by design
DRAM does not need bit lines
SRAM cells require larger capacitors than DRAM
items.explanation: A DRAM cell is just one transistor and one (trench/stacked) capacitor, while an SRAM cell needs six transistors for its cross-coupled latch and access devices. The much smaller per-bit cell area is the entire reason DRAM is denser and cheaper per bit, at the cost of refresh and destructive reads.

items.prompt: On a DDR memory whose I/O bus runs at 1600 MT/s, what is the effective clock frequency of the data strobe driving it?
items.explanation: DDR transfers data on both clock edges, so the megatransfers-per-second figure is twice the clock frequency: 1600 MT/s corresponds to an 800 MHz clock. (Internal prefetch and core arrays run slower still; the 2x edge trick is what gives 'double data rate'.)

items.prompt: Which statement about CAS latency (CL) is correct?
choices: It is the number of clock cycles from a read command to the first valid data word
It is the time the DRAM can hold data without refresh
It is the number of banks in the device
It is the voltage margin on the bit lines
items.explanation: CAS latency is the delay, measured in clock cycles, between issuing the read (column-address) command and the appearance of the first data on the bus. It is a key DRAM timing spec; lower CL at a given data rate means lower read latency. Refresh interval, bank count, and bit-line margin are separate parameters.

lessons.title: Non-Volatile Memory: ROM, EEPROM, and NOR vs NAND Flash
lessons.content:
Non-volatile memory keeps data with power removed. The family tree runs from mask **ROM** (contents fixed at fabrication, cheapest at volume) through **PROM** (one-time fuse programming), **EPROM** (UV-erasable), to **EEPROM** (electrically erasable, byte-addressable read and write). EEPROM is ideal for small, frequently changed data such as calibration constants and serial numbers, but it is expensive per bit.

**Flash** is the dominant non-volatile technology. The storage element is a **floating-gate** (or charge-trap) transistor: charge trapped on an isolated gate shifts the cell's threshold voltage, and that shift is read as a 0 or 1. Flash can be programmed bit-by-bit toward one state but can only be **erased in large blocks**, hence **erase-before-write**: you cannot simply overwrite a byte; the block must be erased first.

Two architectures diverge sharply. **NOR flash** wires cells in parallel for fast random read and full address/data buses, so code can run directly from it, **Execute-In-Place (XIP)**, with no copy to RAM. It is used for boot code and firmware. **NAND flash** strings cells in series, giving far higher density but only **page/block** access (no random byte read), making it the basis of mass storage (SSDs, eMMC, SD cards).

Cells also trade endurance for density: **SLC** (1 bit/cell) is fastest and most durable; **MLC/TLC/QLC** pack 2/3/4 bits per cell at lower cost but with slower writes, weaker error margins, and fewer program/erase cycles. Because every erase wears the oxide, flash needs **wear leveling** (spreading writes across blocks), plus ECC and **bad-block management**, all handled by the flash controller/FTL.

items.front: Order the classic ROM family by programmability: ROM, PROM, EPROM, EEPROM.
items.back: Mask ROM: contents defined at fabrication, not changeable, cheapest in high volume. PROM: one-time programmable by blowing fuses/antifuses in the field. EPROM: erasable by UV light through a quartz window, then reprogrammable (bulk erase). EEPROM: electrically erasable and writable in-circuit, byte-addressable for both read and write, but costly per bit. Each step adds reprogrammability at higher cost per bit.
items.hint: Fixed -> one-time -> UV-erasable -> electrically byte-erasable.

items.front: How does a floating-gate flash cell store a bit?
items.back: The cell is a MOSFET with an extra electrically isolated 'floating' gate buried in the oxide between the control gate and channel. Injecting/removing charge on that floating gate (via tunneling or hot-electron injection) shifts the transistor's threshold voltage. When read, the cell either conducts or not at a given gate voltage, and that threshold shift is interpreted as the stored bit. The charge stays trapped with power off, giving non-volatility. (Modern devices may use a charge-trap layer instead of a conductive gate.)
items.hint: Trapped charge shifts the threshold voltage.

items.front: What is 'erase-before-write' in flash, and why does it exist?
items.back: Flash programming can only move bits in one direction (typically setting them to 0) cell-by-cell, but returning bits to the erased state (1) can only be done a whole block at a time. So you cannot overwrite arbitrary data in place: the block must first be erased (all 1s), then programmed. This block-granular erase is a consequence of the cell/array structure and is why flash needs a translation layer to update small amounts of data efficiently.
items.hint: Program bit-by-bit one way; erase only in big blocks.

items.front: NOR vs NAND flash: cell arrangement, access granularity, and typical use.
items.back: NOR: cells in parallel, full random-access read with address/data buses and fast read of any byte, so code can run directly from it (XIP). Lower density, used for boot/firmware. NAND: cells in series strings, much higher density and cheaper, but read/write only by page and erase by block (no true random byte read). Used for mass storage (SSD, eMMC, SD). NOR favors code execution; NAND favors bulk data.
items.hint: Parallel/random/XIP vs series/page-block/mass-storage.

items.front: What is Execute-In-Place (XIP) and which flash supports it?
items.back: XIP means the CPU fetches and executes instructions directly from the non-volatile memory without first copying them into RAM. It requires fast, random-access reads with a memory-mapped interface, which NOR flash provides. NAND flash cannot do true XIP because it lacks random byte read (page-oriented, needs a buffer), so NAND-based systems copy/boot code into RAM first.
items.hint: Run code straight from NOR; NAND must copy to RAM.

items.front: Compare SLC, MLC, TLC, and QLC flash on bits/cell, speed, endurance, and cost.
items.back: SLC = 1 bit/cell: fastest, highest endurance (most P/E cycles), most expensive per bit. MLC = 2 bits/cell, TLC = 3, QLC = 4: progressively higher density and lower cost per bit, but slower (more program steps and read levels), tighter voltage margins (more ECC needed), and fewer program/erase cycles before wear-out. More bits per cell trades durability and speed for capacity and cost.
items.hint: More bits/cell = cheaper/denser but slower and less durable.

items.prompt: A microcontroller must boot and run its firmware directly from non-volatile memory with no external RAM copy. Which is the natural choice?
choices: NOR flash, because it supports random-access reads and XIP
NAND flash, because it is densest
QLC NAND, for lowest cost per bit
items.explanation: Execute-in-place requires fast random byte reads over a memory-mapped bus, which NOR flash provides. NAND (including QLC) is page-oriented and cannot do true XIP, so it must copy code to RAM first. DRAM is volatile, so it cannot hold boot code with power off.

items.prompt: Why can you not simply rewrite a single byte in NAND flash the way you can in EEPROM?
choices: NAND can program toward one state cell-by-cell but can only erase in large blocks, so an in-place byte change is impossible without erasing the whole block
NAND bytes are read-only after manufacture
NAND requires UV light to write
items.explanation: Flash erase is block-granular: bits can be programmed one way per cell but only returned to the erased state a whole block at a time (erase-before-write). To change one byte, a controller must read-modify-write an entire block (or write to a fresh block and remap), which is why a flash translation layer exists. EEPROM, by contrast, supports true byte erase/write.

items.prompt: What problem does wear leveling solve in a flash-based storage device?
choices: It spreads program/erase cycles across all blocks so no single block wears out prematurely
It increases the read speed of the device
It removes the need for error-correcting codes
items.explanation: Each flash block tolerates only a limited number of program/erase cycles before the oxide degrades. Wear leveling (in the controller/FTL) distributes writes evenly across physical blocks so hot, frequently-updated logical data does not destroy a few blocks while others stay fresh, extending device lifetime. It does not change erase granularity or remove the need for ECC and bad-block handling.

items.prompt: For storing a few hundred bytes of calibration constants that are updated occasionally and must survive power loss, the best fit is:
items.explanation: EEPROM is non-volatile and supports true byte-level erase/write in-circuit, ideal for small, occasionally-updated parameters like calibration data. NAND is block-erased and oversized for a few hundred bytes; SRAM is volatile (loses data on power-off); mask ROM cannot be updated at all.

lessons.title: Memory Organization, Dual-Port, and FIFOs
lessons.content:
A memory chip is specified as **depth x width**: depth is the number of addressable locations (set by the number of address lines, $2^n$), and width is the bits per location (the data-bus width). A 8Kx8 device has $2^{13}=8192$ words of 8 bits, so 13 address lines and 8 data lines, plus control lines (chip-select **CS**, output-enable **OE**, write-enable **WE**).

You build bigger memories from smaller chips two ways. To **widen** the word, place chips side by side sharing the same address and CS but driving different data-bus slices, two 8Kx4 parts make an 8Kx8. To **deepen** (add capacity), stack chips that share the address and data buses; the **high-order address bits** drive a **decoder** whose outputs are the per-chip **chip-selects**, so exactly one chip responds at a time. This **chip-select decoding** is how a 2:4 decoder lets four 8Kx8 chips form a 32Kx8 map, with A14-A13 selecting the bank and A12-A0 selecting the word inside it.
